note:
美国国家航空航天局(NASA)官员周四宣布，他们现在的目标是2021年10月31日发射詹姆斯·韦伯太空望远镜(James Webb Space Telescope)。这比之前宣布的耗资100亿美元的望远镜2021年3月的日期推迟了7个月，这将使科学家能够比以往任何时候都更深入地观察宇宙。
在一次电话会议上，美国国家航空航天局的科学家解释说，最近的延迟主要是由新冠肺炎大流行造成的。不仅NASA和望远镜的主承包商诺斯罗普·格鲁曼(Northrop Grumman)的员工损失了工作时间-他们在大流行爆发前两班工作-新的时间表因素可能会因为病毒而损失更多的时间。
由于时间表中有额外的余量，NASA官员表达了对新日期的信心。美国宇航局韦伯项目主管格雷戈里·罗宾逊(Gregory Robinson)说，我们预计不会超过10月31日。
美国国家航空航天局(NASA)科学项目负责人托马斯·祖尔布钦(Thomas Zurbuchen)表示，NASA已经与欧洲航天局(European Space Agency)就新日期进行了讨论，并确认将在新的发射日期提供阿丽亚娜5号(Ariane 5)火箭。该望远镜将从法属圭亚那的欧洲航天港发射升空。
目前，该望远镜在新的发射日期有三个月的时间表储备，这意味着从现在到明年10月，由于技术或流行病的原因，它可能会损失那么多时间，但仍然可以按时发射。在一个重大项目的这个时间点上，三个月的进度储备是大致正常的，因此，除非发生一些重大灾难或新冠肺炎疫情大幅恶化，否则似乎对新的发射日期有一些信心是合理的。
祖尔布钦指出，在3月份之前的一个季度和大流行爆发之前，韦伯望远镜的工作没有浪费时间。
如果NASA能够坚持现有的时间表，不会遇到重大的额外问题，官员们还相信，该项目现有的预算将弥补延迟的成本-这意味着航天局不需要回到国会要求更多资金。
韦伯望远镜是美国国家航空航天局(NASA)在哈勃太空望远镜(Hubble Space Telescope)基础上的后续设备，原定于大约10年前发射，研发成本为10亿美元。从那时起，技术问题和延误一直困扰着这台复杂的望远镜。
建造韦伯一直很困难，因为它的6.5米长的镜子一旦到达距离地球约150万公里的轨道，就需要展开自己。这是一个极其复杂的过程，天文台上有300多个单点故障。美国国家航空航天局在模拟深空温度、压力和微重力的条件下，在地球上对它们进行了一次艰难的测试。
note_en:
NASA officials announced Thursday they are now targeting a launch date of October 31, 2021, for the James Webb Space Telescope. This represents a seven-month delay from the previously announced March 2021 date for the $10 billion telescope that will allow scientists to observe deeper into the universe than ever before.
 In a teleconference, NASA scientists explained that the latest delay has been driven primarily by the COVID-19 pandemic. Not only have employees at NASA and the telescope's primary contractor, Northrop Grumman, lost work time—they were working two shifts prior to the onset of the pandemic—the new schedule factors in the potential for additional lost time due to the virus.
  Because of the additional margin in the schedule, the NASA officials expressed confidence in the new date. "We're not expecting to go beyond October 31st," said Gregory Robinson, NASA's Webb program director.
 Thomas Zurbuchen, who leads science programs for the space agency, said NASA has talked with the European Space Agency about the new date and confirmed that an Ariane 5 rocket will be available for the new launch date. The telescope will launch from the European spaceport in French Guiana.
 At present, the telescope has three months of "schedule reserve" with the new launch date, meaning it can lose that much time for technical or pandemic reasons between now and next October and still launch on time. Three months of schedule reserve at this point in a major project is about normal, so barring some major calamity or a substantial worsening in the COVID-19 pandemic, it seems reasonable to have some confidence in the new launch date.
 Zurbuchen noted that, in the quarter prior to March and the onset of the pandemic, work on the Webb telescope lost no time.
  If NASA can hold to the existing schedule and encounters no major additional problems, officials also believe the project's existing budget will cover the costs of the delay—meaning the space agency need not return to Congress to ask for more money.
 The Webb telescope, NASA's follow-on instrument to the wildly successful Hubble Space Telescope, was originally due to launch about a decade ago, with a development cost of $1 billion. Since then, technical problems and delays have bedeviled the complex telescope.
 Building Webb has been difficult because its 6.5-meter mirror needs to unfurl itself once it reaches an orbit about 1.5 million kilometers from Earth. This is an exceedingly complex process, and there are more than 300 single points of failure aboard the observatory. NASA has had a difficult time testing them all on Earth in conditions that mimic the temperatures, pressure, and microgravity of deep space.
title: 韦伯太空望远镜的发射又推迟了七个月，直到2021年底
title_en: Webb space telescope launch delayed seven more months to late 2021

note:
得克萨斯州和亚利桑那州的官员已经要求冷藏卡车装载死者，因为医院和停尸房已经被肆虐的新冠肺炎疫情的受害者压垮了。
圣安东尼奥地区Christus Santa Rosa Health System的首席医疗官肯·戴维斯(Ken Davis)在本周的一次简报会上说：“在医院里，可以摆放身体的地方就这么多。”“我们没有地方了，我们的殡仪馆也没有地方了，我们需要那些床位。所以，当有人死的时候，我们需要迅速把床翻过来。
“这是一件很难谈论的事情，”戴维斯补充说。“人们的亲人正在死去。”
总体而言，德克萨斯州新型冠状病毒感染者的住院和死亡人数激增，新感染病例的报告仍在上升。仅在周三，该州就报告了10791例新病例，使病例总数超过28.2万例。到目前为止，该州报告的死亡人数已超过3400人。
该州的其他几个地区已经订购、购买或正在寻找冷藏车或拖车来存放身体。据德克萨斯论坛报报道，这包括努切斯、卡梅伦、特拉维斯、伊达尔戈和哈里斯县，以及奥斯汀市。
亚利桑那州的情况看起来也好不到哪里去，住院和死亡人数也在激增。该州已经统计了超过13.4万例病例和2492例死亡，其90%的床位已经达到了成人重症监护患者的容量。凤凰城市长上周晚些时候表示，该市正在努力确保冷藏车的安全。
类似的情况可能会在佛罗里达州上演，该州也报告了创纪录的病例和死亡人数。最近几天，该州创下历史新高，7月12日有超过15,300例病例，今天有156人死亡。根据医疗管理机构(Agency For Healthcare Administration)的数据，该州约有50家医院的重症监护病房的成人床位已经用完。
严峻的现实呼应了今年早些时候纽约市地区爆发疫情的高峰，当时有数十辆冷藏车，其中一些停在医院外。今年4月，曼哈顿的一名急诊室医生担心，如果我们的国家对大流行的反应得不到改善，卡车将成为一个经久不衰的形象。“每一场灾难都有它的形象，它的象征。对于新冠肺炎来说，可能只是一辆冷藏车。这是我们溢出的停尸房，里面放着我们救不了的病人。“。
到目前为止，美国已经报告了350多万例新冠肺炎大流行，138,000人死亡-比其他任何国家都多。全国每日病例和住院记录目前正在增加。
note_en:
Officials in Texas and Arizona have requested refrigerated trucks to hold the dead as hospitals and morgues become overwhelmed by victims of the raging COVID-19 pandemic.
 “In the hospital, there are only so many places to put bodies,” Ken Davis, chief medical officer of Christus Santa Rosa Health System in the San Antonio area,  said in a briefing this week. “We're out of space, and our funeral homes are out of space, and we need those beds. So, when someone dies, we need to quickly turn that bed over.
 “It’s a hard thing to talk about,” Davis added. “People's loved ones are dying."
 Overall, Texas is seeing a surge in hospitalizations and deaths among people infected with the new coronavirus—and reports of new infections are still on the rise. On Wednesday alone, the state reported  10,791 new cases, bringing the total to over 282,000 cases. The state has reported over 3,400 deaths so far.
 Several other areas in the state have ordered, bought, or are seeking refrigerated trucks or trailers to hold bodies. That includes Nueces, Cameron, Travis, Hidalgo, and Harris counties, as well as the city of Austin,  according to The Texas Tribune.
 The picture is not looking much better in Arizona, were hospitalizations and deaths are also spiking. The state, which has counted over 134,000 cases and 2,492 deaths, has reached 90 percent capacity of its beds for adult intensive care patients. The mayor of Phoenix said late last week that the city is  working to secure refrigerator trucks.
 A similar scenario may play out in Florida, which is also reporting record numbers of cases and deaths. In recent days, the state has record highs, with over 15,300 cases on July 12 and 156 deaths today. Around 50 hospitals in the state have run out of adult beds in their intensive care units,  according to the Agency for Healthcare Administration.
 The grim reality echoes the height of the outbreak in the New York City area earlier this year, which was marked by  dozens of refrigerator trucks, some parked outside hospitals.  In April, one Manhattan-based ER doctor feared that if our national response to the pandemic did not improve, the trucks would become an enduring image. “Every disaster has its images, its symbols. For COVID-19, it might just be the refrigerator truck. It’s our overflow morgue, holding the patients we could not save.”
 So far, the United States has reported more than  3.5 million cases and 138,000 deaths in the COVID-19 pandemic—more cases and deaths than any other country. Nationwide tallies of  daily cases and hospitalizations are currently increasing.
title: 随着停尸房的挤满，德克萨斯州和亚利桑那州转向冷藏车
title_en: With morgues brimming, Texas and Arizona turn to refrigerator trucks

note:
根据上个月提起的一份诉讼，一名被关押在洛杉矶县少管所的16岁男孩在被开出雌激素治疗行为障碍的处方后出现了乳房增大，这一举动让医生们感到困惑，他们认为这种治疗违反了医学逻辑。
起诉书称，这名青少年的身份因年龄原因被隐瞒，他于2019年6月被捕并被关押在东湖少年大厅两天后，被诊断出患有对抗性违抗障碍，或称古怪。“泰晤士报”查阅的医疗记录显示，当诊断他的医生给他开每日剂量的雌激素时，他的睾丸素水平“略高”。
雌激素调节女性性征的发育和生殖。男性分泌的荷尔蒙水平要低得多。
医疗记录显示，在每天服用大约13剂激素后，这名青少年被诊断为女性乳房发育症，定义为雌激素水平过高的男性乳房组织增大或肿胀。
加州大学洛杉矶分校(UCLA)临床精神病学教授詹姆斯·麦高夫(James McGough)表示，注意缺陷多动障碍(ADD)患者有时会出现ODD，这是一种行为状况，通常需要治疗。
“雌激素不是治疗古怪的方法。关于这一点，我再强调不过了。“麦高夫说。“你在任何地方都找不到支持使用雌激素治疗ODD的参考资料。”
诉讼称这种治疗是“试验性的”。记者无法联系到开雌激素处方的医生丹尼·王(Danny Wang)置评。
洛杉矶县的青少年拘留设施由缓刑部门监督。医疗需求由隶属于县卫生服务部的少年法院卫生服务部门提供。在一封电子邮件中，卫生部的一名代表证实，王自2012年以来一直受雇于该县，但拒绝就他目前在该机构的状况发表评论，称这是“机密的人事问题”。该部门拒绝对这起诉讼发表评论。
这起诉讼-将该县的王和少年法院卫生服务医务主任大卫·吴(David Oh)列为被告-指控医疗殴打和玩忽职守。
缓刑官员和这名青少年的律师韦斯利·欧奇(Wesley Ouchi)拒绝透露他被拘留的原因。Ouchi说，这名现年17岁的男孩于4月份出院，将需要手术来治疗他因雌激素治疗而出现的身体问题。
根据医疗记录，王开出了每天2毫克雌激素的处方，以药片的形式服用。直到2019年7月下旬，男孩的父母才意识到他被诊断出患有ODY或正在接受治疗。医生说，没有父母的同意，治疗不应该进行。
为了保护儿子的身份，这名男孩的父亲不愿透露姓名，他说，去年7月的一个周末，他在参观少管所时发现了雌激素药片。
“当我发现他们给他吃避孕药时，我就想，他们为什么不问我？”当我发现这是一种什么样的药片时，我就想，这太可怕了，“父亲说。“他只有16岁，他们强迫他参加考试。”
这位父亲说，他后来在电话中质问了医务主任欧。这位父亲说，哦承认王犯了一个“错误”。由于诉讼正在进行，卫生服务代表拒绝对Oh据称的言论发表评论。
记录显示，去年7月，在这名青少年开始抱怨负面副作用并拒绝药物治疗后，治疗停止了。Ouchi说，在此之前，他的当事人觉得有必要服用这些药片，因为他担心不服从王会对他悬而未决的刑事案件产生不利影响。
Ouchi说，缓刑监督官关于一名青少年被拘留时间的报告在量刑听证会上可能会有很大的影响力，而且在王给他开雌激素的时候，这名青少年的案件还没有判决。
Ouchi还声称，一旦男孩的女性乳房症状出现，他就被其他被拘留的年轻人欺负。
“作为一个十几岁的孩子，他已经感觉到了难为情，”Ouchi说。“经历这些变化让他更加痛苦。”
俄克拉荷马州立大学(Oklahoma State University)儿童与青少年精神病学主任萨拉·科菲(Sara Coffey)表示，ODY通常在6岁至12岁的儿童中被诊断出来。被诊断为ODY的儿童经常在学校或社交场合与权威作斗争。常见的治疗方法包括家庭治疗或在帮助青少年ADHD方面取得成功的药物，包括利他林和阿得拉。
“作为一名精神病学家，我担心的另一个问题是，我们知道荷尔蒙对情绪有影响，”她说。“如果他的情绪被打乱，那只会让事情变得更加复杂。”
据卫生服务代表说，雌激素通常储存在洛杉矶县的青少年设施中，作为避孕和治疗性别焦虑症的激素疗法的一部分。
男孩的父亲说，荷尔蒙疗法对他的儿子产生了长期影响，他现在很容易受到惊吓，变得反社会。
“他就像是另一个人。他只想呆在他的房间里，他不会白白出来，整天呆在他的房间里，“父亲说。“他从来不是那样的人。”
note_en:
A 16-year-old boy being held at a Los Angeles County juvenile hall developed enlarged breasts after he was prescribed estrogen to treat a behavioral disorder, a move that baffled doctors who said the treatment defied medical logic, according to a lawsuit filed last month.
 The teen, whose identity is being withheld because of his age, was diagnosed with oppositional defiant disorder, or ODD, two days after he was arrested and housed at Eastlake Juvenile Hall in June 2019, the lawsuit said. Medical records reviewed by The Times show that the teen’s testosterone levels were “slightly high” when the doctor who diagnosed him prescribed daily doses of estrogen.
 Estrogen regulates the development of female sexual characteristics and reproduction. Men produce the hormone at much lower levels.
 After taking approximately 13 daily doses of the hormone, the teen was diagnosed with gynecomastia, defined as the enlargement or swelling of breast tissue in males whose estrogen level is too high, medical records show.
  ODD, a behavioral condition that is sometimes suffered by patients with attention deficit hyperactivity disorder, is normally treated with therapy, said James McGough, a professor of clinical psychiatry at UCLA.
 “Estrogen is not a treatment for ODD. I can’t be more emphatic about that,” McGough said. “You won’t find a reference anywhere that supports the use of estrogen for ODD.”
 The lawsuit described the treatment as “experimental.” The doctor who prescribed the estrogen, Danny Wang, could not be reached for comment.
 Los Angeles County’s juvenile detention facilities are overseen by the Probation Department. Medical needs are provided by Juvenile Court Health Services, which falls under the county Department of Health Services. In an e-mail, a Department of Health Services representative confirmed that Wang has been employed by the county since 2012 but declined to comment on his current status with the agency, describing it as a “confidential personnel matter.” The department declined to comment on the lawsuit.
    The suit — which names as defendants the county, Wang and David Oh, medical director of Juvenile Court Health Services — alleges medical battery and negligence.
 Probation officials and the teen’s attorney, Wesley Ouchi, declined to say why he was in custody. Ouchi said the boy, now 17, was released in April and will require surgery to treat the physical issues he developed as a result of the estrogen treatment.
 Wang prescribed a daily regimen of 2 milligrams of estrogen to be taken in pill form, according to medical records. The boy’s parents were not aware that he had been diagnosed with ODD or was undergoing treatment until late July 2019. Doctors said the treatments should not have been carried out without the parents’ consent.
 The boy’s father, who spoke on the condition of anonymity to protect his son’s identity, said he found out about the estrogen pills when he visited the juvenile hall one weekend last July.
  “When I found out they were giving him the pill, I was like, why didn’t they ask me? When I found out what kind of pill was it, I was like, this is terrible,” the father said. “He’s only 16, and they were forcing him to take it.”
 The father said he later confronted Oh, the medical director, over the phone. Oh admitted that Wang had made “a mistake,” the father said. The health services representative declined to comment on Oh’s alleged remark due to the ongoing litigation.
 The treatments stopped last July, after the teen began to complain of negative side effects and refused medication, records show. Prior to that, Ouchi said, his client felt compelled to take the pills because he feared that disobeying Wang would have a detrimental effect on his pending criminal case.
 Reports from probation officers about a youth’s time in custody can carry significant weight at sentencing hearings, and the teen’s case had not been adjudicated at the time Wang prescribed him the estrogen, Ouchi said.
  Ouchi also alleges the boy was bullied by other youths in custody once his gynecomastia symptoms developed.
 “As a teenager, he felt self-conscious already,” Ouchi said. “Going through these changes made it a lot more traumatic for him.”
 Sara Coffey, director of child and adolescent psychiatry at Oklahoma State University, said ODD is normally diagnosed in children between the ages of 6 and 12. Children diagnosed with the disorder often struggle with authority, in school or in social settings. Common treatments include family therapy or medications that have had success in aiding juveniles with ADHD, including Ritalin and Adderall.
   “The other concern I have, as a psychiatrist, is that we know hormones play a role in mood,” she said. “If his mood got disrupted, that could only further complicate things.”
 Estrogen is normally stocked at L.A. County juvenile facilities for use as part of hormone therapy for contraception and treatment of gender dysphoria, according to the health services representative.
 The boy’s father said the hormone therapy has had a long-term effect on his son, who now scares easily and has become antisocial.
 “He’s like a different person. He just wants to be in his room, and he don’t come out for nothing, all day in his room,” the father said. “He was never like that.”
title: 16岁的男孩在洛杉矶少管所时因行为障碍接受了雌激素治疗。
title_en: Boy, 16, was given estrogen for behavioral disorder while in L.A. juvenile hall

note:
从流感大流行的早期阶段开始，围绕导致新冠肺炎的病毒突变-以及它们如何可能使其更具传染性-的喧嚣就一直存在。关于一项涉及SARS-CoV-2病原体的“尖峰”的特定突变的预印本研究之前已经引起了人们的注意，这项研究现在已经进行了同行评议，并发表在“细胞”(Cell)杂志上。这篇论文详细描述了病毒中一种氨基酸的变化，这可能使它更具传染性。但病毒学家对这种突变的可能作用远未达成共识。
本文指出，SARS-CoV-2刺突蛋白(SARS-CoV-2刺突蛋白)上由D(天冬氨酸)到G(甘氨酸)的单一氨基酸变化是决定病原体传染性的关键。“尖峰蛋白在病毒生物学中起着至关重要的作用，”洛斯阿拉莫斯国家实验室的计算生物学家、这篇新论文的主要作者贝特·科伯(Bette Korber)说。
科伯和她的同事们在采用多种方法检查了这两种菌株后得出了这一结论。首先，他们进行了一项统计分析，显示变异病毒-通常被称为“G株”-如何在多个大陆获得优势，表现优于共存的SARS-CoV-2原始版本，即“D株”。然后，研究人员在英国谢菲尔德教学医院国民保健系统基金会信托基金与新冠肺炎一起测试了个人体内的病毒数量。结果表明，G株比D株在人体内产生更多的病毒。但前者并没有导致更高的住院率，这意味着它显然没有导致更严重的疾病。最后，研究小组成员将含有D或G氨基酸的SARS-CoV-2刺突蛋白嵌入到其他致病病毒中，从而创造出“假类型”病原体。他们通过在实验室培养皿中感染人类细胞来测试这些假型病毒，结果表明携带G的病毒更具传染性。然而，没有参与这项研究的瑞士巴塞尔大学分子流行病学家艾玛·霍德克罗夫特(Emma Hodcroft)表示，检查培养皿中的细胞与使用“人体免疫系统中的多种细胞类型”测试它们是不同的。“我们只需要非常小心地对待我们得出的结论。”
研究作者说，他们并不是说目前的疫苗和治疗努力将是无用的，这些努力大多是基于原始的D毒株。“但这意味着我们需要仔细检查(突变的影响)，并确保没有影响，”科伯说。“你要确定(疗法和疫苗)对今天的病毒有效，而不仅仅是昨天的病毒。”
与此同时，一些科学家担心这些结果被过度解读了。耶鲁大学的内森·格鲁博和他的同事在科伯研究的同一天在“细胞”杂志上发表了另一篇论文，为其研究结果列出了局限性和可供选择的解释。其他人则在科伯和她的同事的论文作为预印本首次发布时，通过社交媒体对他们的结论进行了辩论。
格鲁博的论文指出，当一种菌株更流行时，并不总是意味着它的传染性更强。研究人员写道，G菌株的较高频率“可能是偶然的”。他们补充说，“有很好的证据表明，对于SARS-CoV-2，少数感染是大多数传播的罪魁祸首”，而且大多数播种爆发的感染可能恰好涉及G毒株。也有可能(在欧洲出现的)毒株传播得更远，更频繁地传播到地球上的其他地方，导致其在全球占据主导地位。霍德克罗夫特说：“这是一种真正困难的解开，”他指出，有太多因素在起作用。
至于在人体内检测到的病毒数量，科伯的团队使用的方法“没有测量传染性病毒，这就是传播的唯一重要因素，”哥伦比亚大学瓦格洛斯内科和外科学院的病毒学家文森特·拉卡涅洛(Vincent Racaniello)说，他也没有参与“细胞”的这两篇论文中的任何一篇。他说，在病毒感染中，“你有一段时间实际上是在产生传染性病毒。但之后感染基本上就停止了。然后剩下的就是在你体内持续很长时间的降解病毒。“。Korber和她的同事使用的技术可以检测到降解的病毒，“所以这不是一个足够好的实验来证明这是驱动传播的，”他补充道。
G菌株是否会影响潜在疫苗和治疗药物的疗效尚不清楚。科伯团队的这篇论文表明，来自感染SARS-CoV-2的人的抗体可以对抗这两种病毒株。“这真的是令人鼓舞的一步，尽管还需要做更多的工作，”她说。
关于新发现的突变及其影响的争论可能会继续下去，因为有几篇预印论文表明G菌株可能更具传染性。但到目前为止，科伯的研究是唯一一项经过同行评审的研究。
霍德克罗夫特说：“科学家之间的这种来回争执真的很正常，科学就是这样进化的。”“这并不是说所有的科学界都存在分歧……。如果你(调查)科学家，他们会给你同样的关于该病毒的大致看法。“。
“我不是说他们的建议不可信，”拉卡涅洛说。“有朝一日，这一点可能会得到证明，我对此并不介意。”
从“科学美国人”这里阅读更多关于冠状病毒爆发的信息。在这里阅读我们的国际杂志网络的报道。
note_en:
The hubbub around mutations in the virus that causes COVID-19—and how they might make it more infectious—has been around since the early phase of the pandemic. A preprint study about a particular mutation involving the “spikes” studding the SARS-CoV-2 pathogen had previously drawn attention, and that investigation has now been peer-reviewed and published in  Cell. The paper details a change in one amino acid in the virus  that may have made it more infectious. But virologists are far from a consensus about the possible role of this mutation.
  The paper indicates that a single amino acid change from D (aspartic acid) to G (glycine) on the SARS-CoV-2  spike protein (which such viruses use to grab onto human cells) is the key to how infectious the pathogen is. “The spike protein has a critically important role in the biology of the virus,” says Bette Korber, a computational biologist at the Los Alamos National Laboratory and lead author of the new paper.
  Korber and her colleagues came to this conclusion after employing multiple approaches to examine the two strains. First, they performed a statistical analysis that showed how the mutated virus—often referred to as the “G strain”—achieved dominance across multiple continents, outperforming the coexisting original version of SARS-CoV-2, or “D strain.” Then the researchers tested the amount of the virus in individuals with COVID-19 at the Sheffield Teaching Hospitals NHS Foundation Trust in England. The results showed that the G strain produced more of the virus in the human body than the D strain. But the former did not lead to a higher hospitalization rate, meaning it apparently did not cause more severe illnesses. Lastly, the team members created “pseudotype” pathogens by embedding SARS-CoV-2 spike proteins, containing either D or G amino acids, into other disease-causing viruses. They tested these pseudotype viruses by infecting human cells in a lab dish, and the result suggested that the G-bearing one was more infectious. Examining cells in a dish, however, is not the same as testing them using “multiple cell types with an immune system in a human body,” says Emma Hodcroft, a molecular epidemiologist at the University of Basel in Switzerland, who was not involved in the study. “We just have to be really careful with how far we take the conclusions.”
  The study authors say they are not arguing that current vaccine and therapeutic efforts, most of which are based on the original D strain, will be useless. “But it means that we need to carefully check [the effects of the mutation] and make sure there is no impact,” Korber says. “You want to be certain [therapies and vaccines] work well against today’s virus, not just yesterday’s virus.”
  Meanwhile some scientists worry the results are being overinterpreted. Nathan Grubaugh of Yale University and his colleagues publishedanother paper in  Cell on the same day as Korber’s study to lay out  limitations to—and alternative explanations for—its findings. Others debated Korber and her colleagues’ conclusions via  social  media when their paper was first released as a preprint.
  Grubaugh’s paper points out that when one strain is simply more prevalent, that does not always mean it is more infectious. The researchers write that the higher frequency of the G strain “may be explained by chance.” They add that “there is good evidence that for SARS-CoV-2, a minority of infections are responsible for the majority of transmission” and that most of the infections that seeded outbreaks may have happened to involve the G strain. It is also possible that strain (which emerged in Europe) traveled farther and more frequently to other parts of the planet, leading to its global dominance. “That’s kind of the disentangling that’s really difficult,” Hodcroft says, noting that there are too many factors at play.
  As for the amount of virus detected in the human body, the method Korber’s team used “doesn’t measure infectious virus, and that’s all that matters for transmission,” says Vincent Racaniello, a virologist at the Vagelos College of Physicians and Surgeons at Columbia University, who was also not involved in the either of the two  Cell papers. In viral infections, he says, “you have a period where you’re actually producing infectious virus. But then the infection basically stops. And then what are left are degraded viruses that persist in your body for a long time.” The technique employed by Korber and her colleagues can detect degraded viruses, “so that’s not a good enough experiment to prove that this is driving transmission,” he adds.
  Whether the G strain could affect the efficacy of potential vaccines and therapeutics is still unknown. The paper by Korber’s team shows that antibodies from people who had been infected with SARS-CoV-2 fight against both strains. “That was a really encouraging step, although more work needs to be done,” she says.
  The debate over the newly identified mutation and its implications will likely continue, because there are several preprint papers that suggest the G strain  might be  more  infectious. But Korber’s study is the only one to do so that has been peer-reviewed so far.
  “These back-and-forths between scientists, they really are normal, and this is how science evolves,” Hodcroft says. “It isn’t an indication that all of science is in disagreement.... If you [surveyed] scientists, they would give you the same general ideas about the virus.”
  “I’m not saying that what they’re suggesting is not plausible,” Racaniello says. “This could be proven one day, and I would be fine with that.”
  Read more about the coronavirus outbreak   from Scientific American  here . And read coverage from our  international network of magazines here.

title: 第二株冠状病毒可能更具传染性，一些科学家对此表示怀疑
title_en: Second Coronavirus Strain May Be More Infectious, Some Scientists Are Skeptical

note:
是拉贾斯坦邦的村民。他们看着我们在塔尔沙漠的火光下经过。我们没有洗澡，身上覆盖着粗糙的灰尘，阳光下一片漆黑：烧焦的稻草人骑着一头运货驴艰难地穿越印度。当地人把我们误认为是流浪表演者、旅行的江湖医生、马戏团游牧民族。他们相信我们是巫师。他们的问题的答案是：是的，当然。我们带着魔法。不过，每个人都是如此。
人类是流动的微咸水井。正如每个小学生都知道的那样，我们的身体包含的水与覆盖地球表面的水的百分比大致相同。这样的和声并不神秘。我们是出生在水行星上的水生动物。水无处不在，又无处可寻。它是一种不安分的元素--在移动中，它的物理状态总是从气体到液体再到固体，然后再变回来。
水分子像箭尖一样弯曲。就像肘部一样。这有助于赋予水一定的极性，即两端都有一个无穷小的电荷。这就是它如何共同塑造我们的现实。它是我们有形世界的神奇溶剂和粘合剂。它是一种化合物，可以溶解和结合我们的脑细胞、山脉、从我们的早茶中飘出的蒸汽，以及构造板块。
然而，这里却几乎没有什么可喝的！咸海容纳了地球上大约97%的水。两极和冰川虽然在气候变化的影响下融化，但锁定了约2%。在世界总供应量中，只有不到百分之一的极小的一滴水可供人类生存：液态淡水。然而，我们却像在沙漠中迷路的傻瓜一样挥霍这笔财富。
我在环游世界。在过去的七年里，我追寻了智人的足迹，他们在石器时代漫步出非洲，探索原始世界。在路上，我收集故事。在我的徒步旅行中，没有任何地方--任何其他国家或大陆--都没有遇到与印度迫在眉睫的水危机规模相当的环境清算。这几乎令人望而生畏，无法思考。
这个世界上人口第二多的国家拥有超过13亿人口，其景观由标志性河流-印度河、恒河、雅鲁藏布江及其所有强大的支流-现在摇摇欲坠，后果不得而知。包括德里、班加罗尔(班加罗尔)和海得拉巴在内的21个印度特大城市的大约1亿人可能会在今年年底前吞下最后一口干涸的地下水。印度北部旁遮普是亚洲重要的粮仓，那里的农民抱怨说，他们的地下水位在一代人的时间里持续超抽，下降了40、60，甚至100英尺。而且问题并不是在供应上就结束了。以工业废物、城市污水和农业径流为形式的污染已经毒害了整个河流系统。总体而言，约有6亿人-大约占印度人口的一半-生活在没有足够清洁水的情况下。与此同时，印度每年有2000万人出生，每个人都需要水才能生存。
我在印度北部的河流平原上跋涉了将近一年半的时间。我在混凝土公路立交桥上跋涉，在铁路桥上保持平衡，坐在颠簸的独木舟中，一河接一河地航行。有几百个。根据印度教的说法，每个人都是神圣的-甚至是神。(恒河，或印地语中的恒河，是一位苍白的女神，画中有多达四只手臂，骑着一条鳄鱼。)。印度的未来在他们的淤泥流中搅动。
孩子们在我们身边蹦蹦跳跳，光着脚，笑着，眯着眼睛看着沙漠的太阳。哨兵海吉里树在黄色的赭色沙子上投下了浅银色的阴影。当地的水井被过量的铁和氟化物毒害。
在Sambhar Salt Lake周围被烧毁的平地上，在斋浦尔郊外一片濒临死亡的湿地上，我们看到数百个衣衫褴褛的身影在远处移动。他们一小时又一小时地往后走，在白色的平原上猛拉着木耙。盐业女工。水银的热气吞噬了它们细长的腿，又把它们送回来了。可怕的胡言乱语。但事实并非如此。在一个没有水的世界里只有我们。
印度-希腊语中的Indos，波斯语中的Hind派生而来，起源于梵语单词Sindhu，意思是河流。
在哪里可以找到这条诞生于西藏冰川上的非常长、强壮的水道-一个巨大、柔软、活的液体实体，其盆地横跨地球近50万平方英里-古代文明的滋养者，印度和巴基斯坦数百万农民的两国生命线？当我走过印度的旁遮普邦时，要找到它并不是一件简单的事情。
我加入了环境摄影师阿拉蒂·库马尔-拉奥(Arati Kumar-Rao)的行列，艰难地走在阿姆利则以南的偏僻小路上。印度河的五条大支流横跨印度西北部。杰勒姆号。切纳布。拉维。比斯一家。萨特莱伊号。我们要找的是比亚斯。很快我们就迷路了。我们跌跌撞撞地走进了工业农业的迷宫。
每一天都是火炉。我们在一望无际、热气腾腾的麦田里汗流浃背。我们路过顶部有通风的白色圆顶的锡克教寺庙，那里的志愿者为所有路人提供简单的豆子和米饭。我们躲避拖拉机隆隆作响的舰队。每个人都通过绑在操作员椅子上的扬声器向天空播放旁遮普流行音乐。为什么？很难说。司机们能在轰鸣的引擎上听到音乐吗？在旁遮普上空飞行的外星人会惊奇地往下看-手指堵住耳朵。聋人的邪教(他们会认为)正在进行一些不知疲倦的仪式：用机器蚀刻土地，为宇宙唱小夜曲。但事实并非如此：他们只不过是工作中的旁遮普农民。
然后，模糊地，我明白了。我们已经找到印度河了！几天几个星期以来，我们一直在弥漫的河流中漫步。它的水流被改道，排干，疏导，扩散，分解成无数的运河、管道、堰和沟壑。这个人类建造的毛细管系统使得印度河支流的古老绿色通道在很大程度上与地理实体无关。旁遮普的数十亿个成熟的麦穗中，每一个都以雾化的形式携带着一滴印度河分水岭。
印度是绿色革命的早期斗士。自20世纪60年代以来，高产种子、化肥和杀虫剂、拖拉机和电动井泵极大地提高了作物产量。印度曾经是饥荒的典范，如今却养活了自己。它的农民向世界出售大量的谷物和水果。但这场战胜饥饿的惊人胜利是以高昂的代价取得的。农业化学品污染了印度河的支流，可能导致癌症等疾病的热点。几十年来不可持续的收成已经到期：有限地下水的惊人损失。在旁遮普，农业是一种冒险。数百万人正在逃离，移民到中东、北美和其他地方。
“很难不感到不知所措，”库马尔-劳(Kumar-Rao)在运河路上用拖拉机拉着房子大小的谷壳发出牢骚。她花了数年时间记录印度水资源的露天开采。“我们的否认是集体失明的一种形式。”Kumar-Rao想要找到另一种盲人生物，濒临灭绝的印度河海豚-Platanista Gangetica Minor-这种著名的海洋哺乳动物的淡水表亲。
“这里再也没有布兰了！”一名衣冠楚楚的男子自称印度斯塔尼少校，在哈里克弹幕附近宣布。Bhulan是印度河海豚的当地名称。
印度斯塔尼少校是个狡猾的摩托车手。他在一家小型巡回马戏团工作。他卷起衬衫袖子展示鼓起的二头肌，为我们表演特技-单腿栖息在他移动的皇家恩菲尔德的座位上-当我们目瞪口呆地看着，在Beas River安静、泥泞、残存的河岸上。漫步印度就是这样的。你会在意想不到的地方遇到各种各样的人物。但是印度斯塔尼少校原来也是瞎子。库马罗发出一声尖叫。她在近海发现了海豚。一头母牛和她的小牛。它们在双鱼光滑的棕色水流中起起落落，发出轻柔的亲吻般的声音，打破了水面。
最近的一项调查表明，生活在双鱼岛的印度河海豚不超过11只。
只要有足够的时间，水几乎可以击败任何东西。斯通。铁。骨头。河流看穿了时间本身的地层学。然而，父权制依然存在。
而不是对少数民族的镇压。而不是根植于宗教的不宽容。而不是收入不平等。不：这是将女性排除在人类奖励和机会的账簿之外。没有一个社会是完全不受影响的。今天活着的70多亿智人中，有一半被剥夺了平等获得政治权力的机会，被迫更加努力地工作，报酬也更少-因为他们有两条X染色体。
独立记者普里扬卡·博尔普贾里(Priyanka Borpujari)说：“不要让我开始。”他参加了拉贾斯坦邦和中央邦风景秀丽的尚巴尔河分水岭的徒步旅行。“我是许多新闻会议上象征性的‘棕色女性问题’撰稿人。我就不能做点别的吗？经济学作家？政治分析家？外国记者？“。
在到达尚巴尔山的粉色砂岩之前，我们在一个稻田停了下来。它完全由女性管理。在睾丸激素泛滥的印度，这很有趣。
“我们在这里办事。这是必须的，“62岁的女族长萨罗伊·德维·亚达夫(Saroj Devi Yadav)说。“所有的男人都去城里工作了。”
亚达夫的丈夫在遥远的斋浦尔送餐。亚达夫和她的两个十几岁的孙女呆在家里给田地浇水。他们切饲料。他们放牧奶牛和水牛。他们把牛奶装在挂在摩托车上的锡罐里运到城里。在附近的农场，情况也大同小异。当太阳落在她小小的绿色领地上时，亚达夫分享着她的茶和咖喱。
“我13岁就结婚了，”她一边说，一边用手挥动着记忆。“那时候情况就不同了。没人问我们女孩。今天，女孩们有了更多的选择。他们结婚的时间比较晚。“。
这是一个古老的故事：城市化的破坏。不同民族在蓬勃发展的特大城市中的碰撞打破了由来已久的性别障碍。然而，在印度，多达三分之二的农业劳动力是女性，只有区区13%的印度女性真正拥有土地。妇女挑起乡村的水。但是印度的自然资源仍然牢牢掌握在男人手中。
尚巴尔河流淌得干干净净。它形成了食人族的避难所，这些食人族是印度的长鼻子鳄鱼。这条河崎岖的源头曾经庇护着印度最著名的女强盗菲奥兰·德维(Phoolan Devi)，她是罗宾汉(Robin Hood)式的人物，据说在一场枪战中杀死了大约20名敌对的持枪歹徒。
这是一个胖子驾驶着一辆昂贵的SUV沿着一条滚烫的柏油路带子行驶。他在我们前面刹车。他挡住了我们的路。他用手机把我们拍到窗外：数百万人中的两个人在印度炎热的路边徘徊。博尔普加里举起一只手。
“我不知道”--男子怒气冲冲地说--“我需要许可。”
博尔普加里站在他的窗户前。她采取了一种好斗的姿态--她后来承认--这是她所憎恨的。她坦率地告诉他，“你需要许可。”
我往东走了几个月。我在印度漫长的金色核心地带穿行。
我的GPS轨迹穿过瘦肉型奶牛带，穿过中央邦和北方邦，穿过那些被时间抛弃的村庄，他们很可能自1947年独立以来就再也没有见过外国人。(“你是英国人吗？”人们会问。)。我睡在路边餐馆的木板桌上，或者睡在农民家里的绳床上，或者睡在清真寺和印度教寺庙的木板桌上。我甚至不知不觉地从一个分水岭走到了另一个分水岭--几千年的耕作使印度布满皱纹的河流平原变得平坦。有几十个。他们现在喂恒河。
在一个叫Seondha的地方，一座巨大的堡垒在信德河一个平静的弯道旁坍塌。高耸的中世纪大门竖立着一英尺长的铁钉，以抵御战象的撞击。建造这个据点的邦德拉·拉杰普特人的最后一个后裔仍然住在一座堡垒里。在漆黑的城墙里露营了一夜，我从未见过他。
在贝特瓦河缓缓的棕色水流旁，我遇到了采砂工人。他们组成了一支衣衫褴褛的瘦子大军，用铲子和机械挖掘机把河床挖出来。沙子可能会用卡车运到300英里外的勒克瑙和新德里的建筑工地。许多采砂作业都是非法的。在印度，沙子是一种有利可图的商品。它助长了建筑热潮和黑市，尽管掠夺破坏了水生栖息地，扰乱了水文，但黑市既受到暴徒的掠夺，也受到暴徒的保护。(联合国的一项研究计算出，人类对不起眼的建筑沙子的胃口越来越大-每年超过400亿吨-是世界河流总和自然补充的沉淀量的两倍。)。采砂黑手党杀害了试图阻止印度河流内脏破坏的执法人员。他们谋杀了揭露挖掘水道这一被禁止做法的记者。
“继续走，”我最新的散步伙伴、河流保护者西德哈斯·阿加瓦尔(Siddharth Agarwal)厉声说道，矿工们大喊着让我们停下来。
我们假装耳聋。我们斜着身子走到贝特瓦河岸边，招呼一个路过的渔夫，把我们的背包扔进他的小艇里，然后划向对岸。我们走进黑暗中-每天行驶25英里，到达一个村庄，在那里，篝火、鼓声和吟诵宣布了一个印度教节日。大吃一惊的庆祝人员欢迎我们。他们准备了木豆和烤肉。他们铺设夏帕，编织的床，用来睡觉。这种条件反射的热情好客在我在印度农村的道路上是普遍的，自青铜时代以来，这片土地就一直接待着徒步朝圣者。阿加瓦尔小心翼翼地问起采砂的事。
黑手党，政客，亲信-他们控制着生活。诚然，剥离到基岩上的贝特瓦河的洪水比以前更不稳定。是的，不可预测的季风-气候变化-已经使农业变得更加边缘。人们必须挖数千个雨水滋养的小池塘来灌溉他们皱巴巴的田地。但政府正在计划一项戏剧性的救援计划：将整条河流肯河(Ken)改道进入贝特瓦河(Betwa)河道，以补充其缩小的流量。
印度已拨出约20亿美元实施一项有争议的河流互联计划：一项大规模的输水计划，提议通过9000多英里的混凝土运河嫁接30条印度主要河流，以缓解水危机。将肯编织到贝特瓦将是一个测试案例。工程师们计划将肯号“多余”的季风流抽走，并将其输送到“更干燥”的贝特瓦号上。这项工程需要几个堤坝和堤坝淹没35平方英里的土地。环保人士在法庭上打了一场官司。
“这些多余的水都到哪里去了？”在附近的潘纳国家公园(Panna National Park)，印度一位著名的自然资源保护者拉古·春达瓦特(Raghu Chundawat)酸溜溜地问我，潘纳国家公园是濒危老虎的保护区。“政府不会分享其流量数据。我认为就连他们也不知道会有什么影响。“
将河神变成水管的一个已知效果是：肯-贝特瓦项目淹没的大部分土地都在老虎保护区内。
我徒步走在恒河母亲河(Ma Ganga)的岸边，直到她绵延数英里的水流向北，像一片闪闪发光的钢片一样穿过黄色平原，直达瓦拉纳西(Varanasi)。印度教最神圣的城市布满了砖灰。数千名工人用大锤和铁撬敲打瓦拉纳西老区的墙壁，为了城市美化计划将古色古香的小巷和不平衡的建筑夷为平地。居民被驱逐。政府给他们现金。很少有人看起来很开心。转世是很难的。
瓦拉纳西在虔诚的印度教徒中被称为喀什，或者说是“至高无上的光芒照耀的地方”。这座圣城的88座石制高山沿着破旧美丽的台阶滚落到恒河。在他们的底部，奉献者在浑浊的河流中洗去罪恶，在含有数百倍安全水平的粪便细菌的水中饮用和洗澡。每年都有数以万计的朝圣者在高止山脉死去并被烧死。在瓦拉纳西火化是获得摩克沙的最可靠方式，逃离生死的痛苦循环。没有污点的死去的婴儿和圣人可以免于焚烧。取而代之的是，他们的身体被绑在漂浮物上，漂浮在下游。或者用石头沉入恒河。
我坐着看着人类的一切-金盏花和粪便的绚丽花环-融合在恒河中。这里的河流充满了骨灰，这是一条巨大的溪流，本身就拒绝清洗。黎明时分，燕子在青铜的空气中飞来飞去。我想起了我的死者和我的战争。瓦拉纳西是一个等待世界创造或毁灭的好地方。或者更好的是，站起来走一走。宣扬巴萨凡纳的虔诚诗篇：
在比哈尔邦，我带着被干旱窒息的儿子散步。在西孟加拉邦，这是水坝匮乏的蒂斯塔(Tista)。阿萨姆邦的传说中的雅鲁藏布江因降雨和灾难性融化的冰川径流而变得肥沃。看起来有一千岁的男男女女提着一篮筐大米，踏着沙洲。越过搁浅的独木舟。经过的稻田在朦胧的阳光下闪闪发光，就像剥离了银背的旧镜子。雅鲁藏布江滑行而过，这是一条1800英里长的水流输送带，从世界的曲线上瀑布而下。背着数十亿条看不见的鱼，村中喧嚣、恐惧的咔嗒作响。
我和悉德哈斯·阿加瓦尔经常在印度东北部被问话。这是时代的标志。巴基斯坦和印度在有争议的穆斯林领土克什米尔问题上再次发生冲突。仇外情绪激增。纳伦德拉·莫迪(Narendra Modi)领导的印度教民族主义政府帮助助长了这一趋势。在阿萨姆邦，我遇到了一位友好的妇女，鲁帕利·比比，她像逃犯一样躲藏起来。为什么？因为她是近一百年前移民到印度的孟加拉穆斯林后裔，可能会被驱逐出境。
40多岁的稻农比比(Bibi)在雅鲁藏布江泛滥平原上用甘蔗盖茅草的家里告诉我：“一名警察把‘外国人通知’带到了我的房子里。”“他说，‘你是个可疑的人。’”
和阿萨姆邦的其他近200万人一样，她被排除在两极分化的国家公民登记册之外。当局不接受她的文件。与此同时，印度政府为宗教难民提供了一条获得公民身份的途径-穆斯林除外。在新冠肺炎大流行的最初几周，近两亿印度穆斯林被右翼印度教政客妖魔化为疾病携带者。据报道，手持板球棒的暴徒袭击了班加卢市的穆斯林。
谁是印第安人？谁不是呢？多元化和世俗的印度甘地和尼赫鲁能在滑入部落民粹主义后幸存下来吗？很难说。当然，环绕印度的河流宇宙在这些问题上保持沉默。
我在夏季季风中艰难地走出印度的最后几英里。紧挨着缅甸边境的曼尼普尔河泛滥着白色。青山说着无边无际的水的嘶嘶声-瀑布的隆隆声，无数小溪的叹息声，雨水在铁皮屋顶上沉重的敲击声。令人振奋的声音。抓着水蛭，我想起了我在印度遇到的最奇怪的河流：萨拉斯瓦提河。吠陀经文中推崇的神话的“迷失之河”。一些科学家认为，它在几千年前就停止了流动，被地震改道，或者可能被气候变化蒸发了。我在拉贾斯坦邦的沙漠里穿过了它所谓的床。由满是灰尘的鹅卵石组成的宽阔的沟壑。热风。一分子水都看不见。被旱灾惊呆的农民告诉我，政府工程师正在附近钻探测试井。他们希望证明这条河是真实存在的。
在OutofEdenWalk.org和Twitter(@paulsalopek)上关注国家地理研究员保罗·萨洛佩克(Paul Salopek)的环游世界之旅。约翰·斯坦迈尔获得了年度世界新闻照片奖和许多其他奖项，他一直在为国家地理记录保罗·萨洛佩克的部分旅程。他为该杂志拍摄了16多个故事。在www.instagram.com/johnstanmeyer上关注他。
致力于保护地球野生地区的非营利性国家地理协会正在帮助娱乐。

note_en:
It is the villagers of Rajasthan. They watch us pass in the hot light of the Thar Desert. We are unwashed, covered in coarse dust, darkened by sun: charred scarecrows trudging across India with a cargo donkey. Local people mistake us for vagabond performers, traveling quacks, circus nomads. They believe we are sorcerers. The answer to their question is: Yes, of course. We carry magic. But then, so does everyone.
  Human beings are mobile wells of mildly salty water. As every schoolchild knows, our bodies contain roughly the same percentage of water that covers the Earth’s surface. Such harmonies are no mystery. We are water animals born onto a water planet. Water is everywhere and nowhere. It is a restless element—unstill, on the move, always shifting its physical state from gas to liquid to solid and back again.
    Water molecules are bent like an arrow tip. Like an elbow. This helps give water a certain polarity, an infinitesimal charge on each end. This is how it collectively shapes our reality. It is the enchanted solvent and glue of our tangible world. It is the compound that both dissolves and binds our brain cells, mountain ranges, the steam wafting from our morning tea, and tectonic plates.
 And yet there is so little to drink! The salty oceans hold roughly 97 percent of all the water on the globe. The poles and glaciers, though melting under the effects of climate change, lock up about 2 percent. Only an absurdly small droplet of the world’s total supply, less than one percent, is available for human survival: liquid fresh water. And yet, we squander this treasure like fools lost in a desert.
 I am walking across the world. Over the past seven years I have retraced the footsteps of  Homo sapiens, who roamed out of Africa in the Stone Age and explored the primordial world. En route, I gather stories. And nowhere on my foot journey—not in any other nation or continent—have I encountered an environmental reckoning on the scale of India’s looming water crisis. It is almost too daunting to contemplate.
  The world’s second most populous country, home to more than 1.3 billion people and a landscape defined by iconic rivers—the Indus, the Ganges, the Brahmaputra, and all their mighty tributaries—now teeters at the edge of a water emergency with unknowable consequences. Roughly a hundred million people in 21 Indian megacities, including Delhi, Bengaluru (Bangalore), and Hyderabad, may gulp their last groundwater dry by the end of this year. Farmers in northern India’s Punjab, an important Asian breadbasket, complain that their relentlessly overpumped water tables are dropping by 40, 60, even a hundred feet in a single generation. And the problem doesn’t end with supply. Pollution in the form of industrial waste, urban sewage, and agricultural runoff has poisoned entire river systems. In total, some 600 million people—roughly half India’s population—live without enough clean water. Meanwhile, 20 million human beings are born every year in India, each requiring water to live.
 I trek for nearly a year and a half across the river plains of northern India. I plod over concrete highway overpasses, balance atop railroad bridges, and sit on my pack in tippy canoes, navigating river after river. There are hundreds. Each one, according to Hinduism, is sacred—a deity even. (The Ganges, or  Ganga in Hindi, is a pale goddess depicted with as many as four arms, riding a crocodile.) The future of India churns within their silty currents.
  Children skip alongside us, barefooted, laughing, squinting up against the desert sun. Sentinel khejri trees throw pale silver shadows onto the yellow ocher sands. The local wells are poisoned by too much iron and fluoride.
   On the burned flats around Sambhar Salt Lake, in a dying wetland outside Jaipur, we spot hundreds of ragged figures moving in the distance. Hour after hour they walk backward, yanking wooden rakes over the white plain. Women salt workers. The quicksilver heat swallows up their spindly legs, delivers them back again. Infernal abracadabra. But it isn’t, really. It’s just us in a waterless world.
   India—from  indos in Greek, derived from  hind in Persian, originating from the Sanskrit word  sindhu, meaning river.
  Where can one locate this immensely long, brawny waterway, born in the glaciers of Tibet—a gigantic, supple, living, liquid entity whose basin sprawls across nearly half a million square miles of the Earth—a nurturer of ancient civilizations, a binational lifeline for millions of farmers in India and Pakistan? As I walk across the Indian state of Punjab, finding it is no simple task.
  I join Arati Kumar-Rao, an environmental photographer, slogging the back roads south of Amritsar. Five large tributaries of the Indus ribbon across northwestern India. The Jhelum. The Chenab. The Ravi. The Beas. The Sutlej. We seek out the Beas. Soon we are lost. We blunder into a labyrinth of industrial agriculture.
 Each day is a furnace. We sweat around endless, steaming quadrangles of wheat. We pass Sikh temples topped with airy white domes, where volunteers offer simple meals of dal and rice to all passersby. We dodge armadas of chugging tractors. Each blasts Punjabi pop music at the sky through loudspeakers lashed to the operator’s chair. Why? It’s impossible to say. Can the drivers hear the music over their roaring engines? Aliens flying above Punjab would look down in wonder—with fingers plugging their ears. Cults of deaf humans (they would think) are performing some tireless ritual: etching the land in circles with machines, serenading the cosmos. But no: They are simply Punjabi farmers at work.
 And then, dimly, I understand. We have found the Indus already! For days—weeks—we have been walking within the diffused presence of the river. Its currents have been diverted, bled off, channeled, diffused, parsed into countless canals, pipes, weirs, and furrows. This human-built capillary system has rendered the ancient green channels of the Indus tributaries largely irrelevant as geographical entities. Each of Punjab’s billions of ripe wheat heads carries a drop of the Indus watershed in atomized form.
  India was an early warrior in the green revolution. High-yield seeds, fertilizers and pesticides, tractors, and motorized well pumps have hugely increased crop yields since the 1960s. Once the poster child for famines, India feeds itself today. Its farmers sell the world torrents of grains and fruits. But this stunning victory against hunger has come at a steep cost. Agricultural chemicals pollute the tributaries of the Indus, possibly contributing to hot spots of diseases such as cancer. And the bill has come due for decades of unsustainable harvests: a staggering loss of finite quantities of groundwater. Farming is chancy in Punjab. Millions are fleeing, emigrating to the Middle East, North America, elsewhere.
 “It’s hard not to feel overwhelmed,” Kumar-Rao hollers on a canal road whining with tractors pulling house-size bags of chaff. She’s spent years documenting the strip-mining of India’s water resources. “Our denial is a form of mass blindness.” Kumar-Rao wants to find another blind creature, the endangered Indus River dolphin— Platanista gangetica minor—a freshwater cousin of the famed sea mammal.
 “There are no  bhulan here anymore!” a dapper man calling himself Major Hindustani declares near the Harike Barrage. Bhulan is the local name for the Indus River dolphin.
 Major Hindustani is a trick motorcycle rider. He works with a small traveling circus. With shirtsleeves rolled to display bulging biceps, he performs stunts for us—perching one-legged on the seat of his moving Royal Enfield—as we watch, stunned, on a quiet, muddy, relict bank of the Beas River. Walking India is like this. You meet all sorts of characters in unlikely places. But Major Hindustani turns out to be blind too. Kumar-Rao emits a squeal. She spots dolphins offshore. A cow and her calf. They rise and fall in the glossy brown currents of the Beas, breaking the surface with a sound like a soft kiss.
 A recent survey suggests that no more than 11 Indus River dolphins live in the Beas.
    Given enough time, water defeats almost anything. Stone. Iron. Bone. Rivers saw through the stratigraphy of time itself. Yet patriarchy endures.
  Not the suppression of ethnic minorities. Not intolerance rooted in religion. Not income inequality. No: It is the exclusion of women from humanity’s ledger of rewards and opportunities. No society is completely immune. Half of the well over seven billion  Homo sapiens alive today are denied equal access to political power, made to work harder, and compensated less—because they have two X chromosomes.
 “Don’t get me started,” says Priyanka Borpujari, an independent reporter who joins the walk through the scenic Chambal River watershed in Rajasthan and Madhya Pradesh. “I’m the token ‘brown women’s issues’ writer at many journalism conferences. Can’t I be something else? An economics writer? A political analyst? A foreign correspondent?”
   Before reaching the pink sandstone of the Chambal hills, we pause at a rice farm. It’s managed exclusively by women. In testosterone-sodden India, this is interesting.
 “We run things here. It is a necessity,” says Saroj Devi Yadav, the flinty, 62-year-old matriarch. “All the men are away working in the city.”
 Yadav’s husband delivers restaurant food in distant Jaipur. Yadav and her two teenage granddaughters stay home to water the fields. They cut fodder. They herd the cows and buffalo. They organize shipments of milk to the city in tin cans slung across motorbikes. It is much the same at nearby farms. As the sun drops over her tiny green domain, Yadav shares her tea and curry.
 “I got married at 13,” she says, flicking away the memory with her hand. “Things were different then. Nobody asked us girls. Today the girls get many more choices. They marry later.”
  It is an old story: the disruption of urbanization. The collision of diverse peoples in booming megacities cracks open age-old gender barriers. Yet in India, where up to two-thirds of the agricultural workforce are women, barely 13 percent of Indian women actually own land. Women carry the countryside’s water. But India’s natural resources remain cupped firmly in the hands of men.
 The Chambal flows clean. It forms a sanctuary for gharials, the long-snouted crocodilians of India. The river’s craggy headwaters once sheltered India’s most famous woman bandit, Phoolan Devi, a Robin Hood figure who is said to have killed some 20 rival gunmen in a shootout.
  It’s a fat man steering an expensive SUV along a hot ribbon of blacktop. He brakes in front of us. He blocks our way. He films us out his window with a phone: two people among millions wandering the parched roadsides of India. Borpujari raises a hand.
  “I didn’t know”—the man huffs—“that I needed permission.”
 Borpujari plants herself at his window. She assumes a combative stance that—she later admits—she hates. She tells him levelly, “You need permission.”
   I walk east for months. I move through the long golden core of Indian afternoons.
 My GPS track unspools across the lean cow belt, through Madhya Pradesh and Uttar Pradesh, threading hamlets so forsaken by time they likely haven’t seen a foreigner since independence in 1947. (“Are you an Englishman?” people ask.) I sleep on plank tables at roadside eateries called  dhabas, or in rope beds in farmers’ homes, or at mosques and Hindu temples. Without even knowing it—India’s wrinkled river plains are smoothed by millennia of plowing—I inch from one watershed to another. There are dozens. They now feed the Ganga.
 At a place called Seondha, an enormous fortress crumbles beside a placid bend of the Sindh River. The towering medieval gates bristle with foot-long iron spikes: defense against ramming by war elephants. A last descendant of the Bundela Rajputs who built the stronghold still lives in a rampart. Camped within its darkened walls for a night, I never see him.
  By the sluggish brown currents of the Betwa River, I meet sand miners. They form a ragged army of lean men scooping out the riverbed with shovels and mechanical excavators. The sand may be trucked to construction sites as far as Lucknow and New Delhi, some 300 miles away. Many sand-mining operations are illegal. Sand is a lucrative commodity in India. It fuels a building boom, and a black market, that is both preyed upon and protected by goons, even as the plundering destroys aquatic habitats and disrupts hydrology. (A UN study calculates that humankind’s growing appetite for humble construction sand—more than 40 billion tons a year—is double the volume of sediments being replenished naturally by the sum of the world’s rivers.) Sand-mining mafiosi have killed law enforcement officers who’ve tried to halt the gutting of India’s rivers. They’ve murdered reporters who have exposed the forbidden practice of excavating waterways.
 “Keep walking,” snaps my latest walking partner, river conservationist Siddharth Agarwal, as the miners shout at us to stop.
 We feign deafness. We lope down to the Betwa’s banks, hail a passing fisherman, fling our rucksacks into his dinghy, and paddle to the opposite side. We walk into the dark—cranking a 25-mile day to reach a village where bonfires, drums, and chanting announce a Hindu festival. The astonished celebrants welcome us. They prepare dal and roti. They lay out  charpais, woven beds, for sleeping. This reflexive hospitality is universal along my path in rural India, a land that’s hosted foot pilgrims since the Bronze Age. Agarwal asks, gingerly, about sand mining.
   Mafiosi, politicians, cronies—they control life. True, the Betwa, stripped to its bedrock, floods more erratically than before. And yes, the unpredictable monsoons—climate change—have made farming even more marginal. People must dig thousands of small, rain-fed ponds to water their puckered fields. But the government is planning a dramatic rescue: diverting an entire river, the Ken, into the Betwa’s channel to replenish its shrunken flow.
  India has earmarked some $2 billion to implement a controversial interlinking-of-rivers scheme: a massive water transfusion program that proposes to graft 30 major Indian rivers through more than 9,000 miles of concrete canals to ease the water crisis. Braiding the Ken to the Betwa will be the test case. Engineers plan to siphon off the Ken’s “excess” monsoonal flows and funnel them to the “drier” Betwa. Several dams and barrages flooding 35 square miles of land are needed for this engineering to work. Environmentalists delivered a court battle.
 “Where is all of this excess water?” Raghu Chundawat, a leading Indian conservationist, asks me sourly in nearby Panna National Park, a sanctuary for endangered tigers. “The government won’t share its flow data. I don’t think even they know what the impacts will be.”
 One known effect of turning the river gods into plumbing pipes: Most of the land submerged by the Ken-Betwa project lies within the tiger reserve.
   I hike the banks of  Ma Ganga—Mother Ganges—until her milewide currents arc north, cutting like a shining steel blade across the yellow plains to Varanasi. Hinduism’s holiest city is clouded in brick dust. Thousands of workers pummel the walls of Varanasi’s Old Quarter with sledgehammers and crowbars, leveling antique alleyways and lopsided buildings for an urban beautification plan. Residents are evicted. The government gives them cash. Few appear happy. Reincarnation is hard.
 Varanasi is known among devout Hindus as Kashi, or the place “where the supreme light shines.” The holy city’s 88 stone ghats tumble down to the Ganga in beautifully worn steps. At their bottom, devotees wash away sins in murky river currents, drinking and bathing in water that contains hundreds of times the safe levels of fecal bacteria. Tens of thousands of pilgrims each year come to die and be burned at the ghats. To be cremated in Varanasi is the surest way to achieve  moksha, escape from the painful cycle of life and death. Dead babies and holy men without stain are exempted from the pyres. Their bodies instead are tied to flotsam and floated downriver. Or sunk in the Ganga with stones.
 I sit and watch everything human—the brilliant garlands of marigolds and the feces—merge in the Ganga. The river is inky here with bone ash, a colossal stream that itself resists cleansing. At dawn, swallows spear the bronze air. I think of my dead and my wars. Varanasi is a good place to await the creation or destruction of the world. Or better, to get up and walk. Proclaim the devotional poems of Basavanna:
       In Bihar I walk the drought-strangled Son. In West Bengal, it’s the dam-starved Tista. The fabled Brahmaputra in Assam runs fat with rains and the runoff from disastrously melting glaciers. Men and women who look a thousand years old tread its sand banks, carrying baskets of rice. Past beached canoes. Past paddy fields shining in the hazy sunlight like old mirrors with their silver backing peeled off. The Brahmaputra slides by, a 1,800-mile conveyor of water that cascades over the curve of the world. Carrying billions of invisible fish, the click and hum of village noise, fear.
  Siddharth Agarwal and I are questioned often in northeastern India. It’s a sign of the times. Pakistan and India have clashed again over the contested Muslim territory of Kashmir. Xenophobia spikes. The Hindu nationalist government of Narendra Modi helps stoke it. In Assam I meet a friendly woman, Rupali Bibi, who hides like a fugitive. Why? Because she, a descendant of Bangladeshi Muslims who migrated to India nearly a hundred years ago, may be deported.
 “A policeman brought a ‘foreigner notice’ to my house,” Bibi, a rice farmer in her 40s, tells me in her cane-thatched home on the floodplain of the Brahmaputra. “He said, ‘You are a suspicious person.’”
  Like nearly two million others in the state of Assam, she has been excluded from the polarizing National Register of Citizens. The authorities don’t accept her documents. The Indian government, meanwhile, offers a path to citizenship for religious refugees—barring Muslims. And during the early weeks of the COVID-19 pandemic, nearly 200 million Indian Muslims are demonized as disease carriers by right-wing Hindu politicians. Mobs armed with cricket bats reportedly target Muslims in Bengaluru.
 Who is Indian? Who isn’t? Can the diverse and secular India of Gandhi and Nehru survive a slide into tribal populism? It is impossible to say. The cosmos of rivers webbing India, of course, is mute on such matters.
 I slog my last miles out of India through the summer monsoon. The rivers of Manipur, hard by the Myanmar border, rage white. Green hills speak the sibilant language of unbounded water—the rumble of waterfalls, the sighing of countless streams, the hard-knuckled rap of rain on tin roofs. Exhilarating sounds. Plucking at leeches, I recall the strangest river I encountered in India: the Saraswati. A “lost river” of myth exalted in Vedic scriptures. Some scientists believe it stopped flowing thousands of years ago, diverted by an earthquake or perhaps evaporated by climate change. I crossed its supposed bed in the desert of Rajasthan. A broad gully of dusty cobbles. A hot wind. Not a molecule of water visible. Drought-stunned farmers told me that government engineers were boring test wells nearby. They hoped to prove the river was real.
 Follow National Geographic Fellow Paul Salopek’s walk around the world at  OutofEdenWalk.org and on Twitter  (@PaulSalopek). Recipient of the World Press Photo of the Year award and many others,  John Stanmeyer has been documenting parts of Paul Salopek’s journey for  National Geographic. He has photographed more than16 stories for the magazine. Follow him at  www.instagram.com/johnstanmeyer.
 The nonprofit National Geographic Society, working to conserve Earth’s wild places, is helping fun
......
title: 印度的艰巨挑战：到处都是水，却无处可寻
title_en: India’s daunting challenge: There’s water everywhere, and nowhere

note:
全美低收入住房联盟(National Low Income Housing Coalition)年度报告“触不可及”(Of Reach)显示，在美国任何地方，全职最低工资工人都负担不起两居室的租金，在美国95%的县，他们也负担不起一居室的租金。
事实上，根据NLIHC的计算，美国平均最低工资工人每周需要工作近97个小时才能负担得起公平的市场价格：两间卧室，每周79小时，才能买得起一居室。这远远超过了两份全职工作，仅仅是为了付得起一套两居室的租金。
周二发布的这份报告将负担得起的房租定义为不超过月收入的30%，这与大多数预算专家的建议一致。在全国范围内，NLIHC认为2020年的住房工资--也就是全职员工在不超过收入的30%的情况下支付公平的市场租金所必须挣的工资--两居室的租金为每小时23.96美元，一居室的租金为每小时19.56美元。
报告称，这意味着即使是平均每小时挣18.22美元的小时工也付不起房租。许多被认为在猪冠状病毒大流行期间必不可少的工人的收入甚至更低。杂货店收银员的工资中位数为每小时11.61美元，而建筑清洁工和家庭健康及个人护理助理的平均工资为12.94美元。
甚至在大流行来袭之前就已经出现了住房负担能力危机，数千万人失去了工作。去年，NLIHC的报告还发现，在美国任何地方都买不起两居室，2019年的住房工资为两居室租房22.96美元，一居室18.65美元。
但这些发现现在尤其相关。根据美国城市研究所(Urban Institute)的数据，与冠状病毒相关的工作岗位流失对酒店业和服务业的打击尤其严重，租房者在这些劳动力中所占的比例不成比例。此外，美国不太可能实现经济学家们所希望的V型复苏，因为随着病毒病例在全国范围内激增，各州正在重新关闭大部分经济领域。
在所有这一切的背景下，住房专家预测，7月底，住房市场将迎来一场启示录：在疫情爆发之初实施的驱逐禁令正在解除，就像增加的失业救济金即将到期一样。在此背景下，住房专家们预测，7月底即将到来的住房启示录：在疫情爆发之初实施的驱逐禁令正在解除，就像增加的失业救济金到期一样。这可能导致数百万家庭面临驱逐，并可能无家可归，因为他们在支付房租和支付食品和药品等基本生活费用之间做出选择。截至本月初，估计有32%的家庭-近三分之一-错过了住房付款。
报告称，先前存在的结构性不公平，比如有限的住房选择，意味着在疫情大流行之前，美国经历无家可归的人中，有色人种已经占到了不成比例的比例。报告称，2019年经历无家可归的美国人中，黑人占40%，尽管他们只占总人口的13%。这份报告称，在美国，有色人种人在无家可归者中所占比例已经不成比例。据该报告称，在2019年经历无家可归的人中，黑人占40%，尽管他们只占总人口的13%。西班牙裔和拉丁裔人口占总人口的18%，无家可归者占22%。
NLIHC的报告呼吁在负担得起的租赁住房和紧急租赁援助方面投入大量资金，以保持低收入租房者在疫情期间和之后的稳定住房。
众议院已经通过了美国卫生和经济复苏综合应急解决方案法案(HEROES Act)和2020年紧急住房保护和救济法案，以解决这场危机。预计这两项法案都不会在共和党控制的参议院获得通过。
note_en:
Full-time minimum wage workers cannot afford a two-bedroom rental anywhere in the U.S. and cannot afford a one-bedroom rental in 95% of U.S. counties, according to the National Low Income Housing Coalition's annual  "Out of Reach" report.
 In fact, the average minimum wage worker in the U.S. would need to work almost 97 hours per week to afford a fair market rate two-bedroom and 79 hours per week to afford a one-bedroom, NLIHC calculates. That's well over two full-time jobs just to be able to afford a two-bedroom rental.
  The report, released Tuesday, defines "affordable" as spending no more than 30% of monthly income on rent,  in line with what most budgeting experts recommend. Nationally, NLIHC puts the "housing wage" for 2020 — or what a full-time worker must make in order to afford a fair market rental without spending more than 30% of his or her income — at $23.96 per hour for a two-bedroom rental and $19.56 per hour for a one-bedroom.
 That means even the average hourly worker who earns $18.22 per hour cannot afford rent, the report says.  Many workers deemed essential during the coronavirus pandemic earn even less. "Grocery store cashiers earn a median wage of $11.61 per hour, while building cleaning workers and home health and personal care aides earn $12.94," per NLIHC.
  There was a housing affordability crisis even before the pandemic hit and tens of millions lost their jobs. Last year, the  NLIHC's report also found that a two-bedroom was not affordable anywhere in the country, putting the 2019 housing wage at $22.96 for a two-bedroom rental and $18.65 for a one-bedroom.
 But the findings are especially relevant now.  Coronavirus-related job losses hit the hospitality and service industries particularly hard, and renters make up a disproportionate share of those work forces, according to the  Urban Institute. Additionally, it is not likely that the U.S. will make the "V-shaped" recovery economists are hoping for, as states are re-closing large parts of their economies as virus cases spike across the country.
 With all of that as the backdrop, housing experts forecast a  coming housing "apocalypse" at the end of July:  Eviction bans put in place at the start of the pandemic are lifting, just as  enhanced unemployment benefits expire. That could lead  millions of households to face eviction and potentially homelessness as they choose between covering rent and basics like food and medicine. An estimated 32% of households — almost one-third —  missed their housing payments at the beginning of this month.
  "Pre-existing structural injustices," like  limited housing options, mean people of color already made up a disproportionate share of people experiencing homelessness in the U.S. before the pandemic, the report says. Black Americans comprised 40% of people experiencing homelessness in 2019, even though they are just 13% of the population, according to the report. Hispanic and Latino people were 18% of the population and 22% of those experiencing homelessness.
 NLIHC's report calls for "significant investments" in affordable rental housing and emergency rental assistance "to keep low-income renters stably housed during and after the pandemic."
 The House of Representatives has passed the  Health and Economic Recovery Omnibus Emergency Solutions, or HEROES Act, and the  Emergency Housing Protections and Relief Act of 2020 to address the crisis. Neither bill is expected to pass the Republican-controlled Senate.
title: 最低工资工人在美国任何一个州都负担不起房租
title_en: Minimum wage workers cannot afford rent in any U.S. state

note:
大约一年前，微软发布了Xbox Series X(当时被称为斯嘉丽项目)，部分原因是强调了该系统广泛的向后兼容性：你的游戏、你的成就、你的进步、你的配件，以及你使用Xbox的游戏机体验：所有这些都是斯嘉丽带来的。不过今天，微软强调了这条普遍的向后兼容性规则的一个轻微例外：Kinect硬件和为之设计的游戏。
微软Xbox One负责人菲尔·斯宾塞(Phil Spencer)在一篇关于公司Xbox计划(包括即将推出的XCloud与Game Pass的整合)的长篇博客文章中写道，这是我们在发布Xbox One游戏机时，所有不需要Kinect在Xbox Series X上玩游戏的Xbox One游戏的目标。斯宾塞后来向The Verge证实并澄清了这一说法，他直截了当地表示，Kinect无法在X系列上运行。
从某种意义上说，今天的确认并不令人大吃一惊。我们几个月前就知道，X系列缺少了Xbox One的专有Kinect接口(Xbox One也从2016年的Xbox One S和2017年的Xbox One X中去掉了)。但微软在2018年之前为Xbox One版的Kinect提供了USB适配器，第三方配件制造商仍然为硬件提供类似的USB解决方案(Xbox 360版本的Kinect专为USB设计，但需要附带电源适配器才能与较旧版本的游戏机配合使用)。
从某种意义上说，简单地让这些适配器与Xbox系列X兼容可能不需要太多的工作。但这将意味着微软可能没有兴趣承担的仿真开发、测试和支持工作，因为微软自2017年末以来就没有生产过这款设备，而且自2014年以来也没有与Xbox One硬件捆绑在一起。
实际上，缺乏对X系列Kinect的支持只会影响Xbox One上百款游戏库中的几十款游戏。在这些游戏中，一直很受欢迎的Just Dance系列很可能是仍在许多Xbox玩家中活跃轮换的系列，但这些游戏多年来一直允许玩家使用智能手机作为控制器。
在这篇博客文章的其他地方，微软澄清说，Xbox Elite控制器和Xbox Adaptive Controller等专门的Xbox One输入仍然可以在X系列上使用，这可能与更多的Xbox One用户更直接相关。但我们仍然感到有点沮丧的是，微软令人钦佩的全面向后兼容承诺遗漏了Xbox最不寻常和最令人难忘的配件之一。
note_en:
Roughly one year ago, Microsoft revealed the Xbox Series X (then referred to as "Project Scarlett") in part by  highlighting the system's  extensive backward compatibility: "Your games, your achievements, your progression, your accessories, your console experience with Xbox: it all comes forward with Scarlett." Today, though, the company emphasized one slight exception to that general backward-compatibility rule: Kinect hardware and the games designed for it.
 "It's our intent for all Xbox One games  that do not require Kinect to play on Xbox Series X at the launch of the console [emphasis added]," Microsoft Head of Xbox Phil Spencer wrote in a sentence buried in  an extensive blog post about the company's Xbox plans (including  coming xCloud integration with Game Pass). Spencer later confirmed and clarified that statement to The Verge, saying point-blank that "There's no way for Kinect to work" on the Series X.
 In a sense, today's confirmation isn't a big surprise. We've known for months that the Series X is  missing the proprietary Kinect port found on the original Xbox One (which was  also removed from 2016's Xbox One S and 2017's Xbox One X). But Microsoft offered a USB adapter for the Xbox One edition of the Kinect  until 2018, and third-party accessory makers  still offer similar USB solutions for the hardware (the Xbox 360 version of Kinect was designed for USB but required an included power adapter to work with older versions of the console).
 In a sense, it probably wouldn't have taken too much work to simply let those adapters works with the Xbox Series X as well. But that would have meant emulation development, testing, and support work that Microsoft likely isn't interested in undertaking for a device that  it hasn't produced since late 2017 and  hasn't bundled with the Xbox One hardware since 2014.
 In practice, the lack of Series X Kinect support only affects  a few dozen games from the Xbox One's library of hundreds of titles. Of those, the ever-popular  Just Dance series is likely to be the one still in active rotation among many Xbox players, but those games have let players use a smartphone as a controller  for years now.
 Elsewhere in the blog post, Microsoft clarifies that specialized Xbox One inputs like the Xbox Elite Controller and Xbox Adaptive Controller will still work with the Series X, which is probably more directly relevant to more Xbox One users. But we're still a little bummed that Microsoft's admirable commitment to "full" backward compatibility is leaving out one of the Xbox's most unusual and memorable accessories.
title: Xbox系列X不支持Kinect硬件、游戏
title_en: Xbox Series X won’t support Kinect hardware, games

note:
雄心勃勃的Netflix发生了什么？你可能会预计这家公司会报告增长，因为其本土市场的许多消费者都被限制在家里。该公司不仅未能提供投资者一致感到兴奋的第二季度数据，而且还设法预测了弱于预期的业绩。
也许任何一个都是可以接受的，但不是两个都可以接受。以下是Netflix告诉我们的：
Netflix财报：第二季度营收61.5亿美元，营业收入13.6亿美元，净收入7.2亿美元。按每股计算，该公司在这三个月内盈利1.59美元。
根据雅虎财经分析师平均数据，投资者此前预计营收为60.8亿美元，每股收益为1.81美元。因此，Netflix确实在收入上略有起色，但在利润方面却严重失利。
在净客户增加方面，该公司也超出了预期，CNBC报道称，Netflix的1009万新订户超过了826万的预期。
不是最坏的结果，对吧？要完全理解该公司的股价回调，我们必须向前看Netflix对第三季度的说法：
Netflix的预测：收入63.3亿美元，运营收入12.5亿美元，净收入9.54亿美元。按每股计算，该公司预计将获得2.09美元的收入。
该公司还预计第三季度将净增加250万新用户。由于市场原本预计该公司第三季营收为63.9亿美元，每股获利为2.00美元，因此我们再次略显喜忧参半。但与Netflix收入增长慢于预期相关的适度净订户增长似乎吓坏了华尔街。
由于担心Netflix的增长可能会低于预期，其股价应声下跌。也许该公司对净用户增长的预测过于保守，但投资者似乎并不想对其持怀疑态度。
note_en:
What happened to the high-flying Netflix, a company that you might have expected to report growth bolstered by the fact that many consumers in its home market are confined to their homes? The company not only failed to produce Q2 numbers that investors were uniformly excited about, but also managed to forecast weaker performance than anticipated.
 Perhaps either would have been acceptable, but not both. Here’s what Netflix told us:
  Netflix’s results: $6.15 billion in Q2 revenue generated operating income of $1.36 billion and net income of $720 million. In per-share terms, the company earned $1.59 in the three-month period.
 Investors had expected $6.08 billion in revenue and earnings per share of $1.81,  according to Yahoo Finance analyst averages. So, Netflix did manage a slim beat on revenue, but missed sharply in profit-terms.
 The company also beat expectations in terms of net customer adds, with  CNBC reporting that Netflix’s 10.09 million new subscribers bested estimates of 8.26 million.
  Not the worst results, right? To fully understand the company’s share price correction, then, we’ll have to look ahead to what Netflix said about Q3:
 Netflix’s forecasts: $6.33 billion in revenue leading to operating income of $1.25 billion and net income of $954 million. In per-share terms, the company expects to earn $2.09 in revenue.
 The company also expects to add 2.5 million net new subscribers in Q3. As the market had expected the company to generate $6.39 billion in third-quarter revenue and $2.00 in per-share profit, we again have a slightly mixed picture. But the modest net subscriber adds tied to Netflix’s slower-than-anticipated revenue growth appear to have spooked the street.
 And with fear in the air that Netflix’s growth could fall under expectations, down went its share price. Perhaps the company is being conservative with its net subscriber add forecast, but investors didn’t seem to want to give it the benefit of the doubt.
title: 为什么Netflix的股价下跌了10%

note:
加州理工大学微生物学家发现了以锰为食并以锰为卡路里来源的细菌。这样的微生物在一个多世纪前就被预测存在了，但到目前为止还没有发现或描述过任何一种微生物。加州理工大学环境微生物学教授贾里德·利德贝特(Jared LeadBetter)表示，这些是第一批发现以锰为燃料的细菌。他与博士后学者杭宇(Hang Yu)合作，在7月16日的“自然”(Nature)杂志上描述了这一发现。自然界中微生物的一个奇妙的方面是，它们可以代谢看似不太可能的物质，如金属，产生对细胞有用的能量。
这项研究还显示，细菌可以利用锰将二氧化碳转化为生物质，这一过程被称为化学合成。此前，研究人员知道有细菌和真菌可以氧化锰，或者剥夺锰的电子，但他们只是推测，尚未被识别的微生物可能能够利用这一过程来推动生长。在此之前，研究人员已经知道了可以氧化锰或剥夺锰的电子的细菌和真菌，但他们只是推测，尚未鉴定的微生物可能能够利用这一过程来推动生长。
LeadBetter在使用一种轻的、粉笔状的锰进行了无关的实验后，偶然发现了这种细菌。在离开加州理工学院去校外工作几个月之前，他把一个沾满这种物质的玻璃瓶留在了加州理工学院办公室的水槽里浸泡在自来水中。当他回来时，罐子里覆盖着一层深色的材料。
我想，那是什么？他解释道。我开始怀疑是否长期被追捧的微生物是罪魁祸首，所以我们系统地进行了测试，以找出答案。
事实上，黑色的涂层是由新发现的细菌产生的氧化锰，这些细菌很可能来自细菌本身。他说，有证据表明，这些生物的近亲生活在地下水中，帕萨迪纳的部分饮用水是从当地含水层抽出的。
锰是地球表面最丰富的元素之一。氧化锰以黑色块状物质的形式存在，在自然界中很常见；它们在地下沉积物中被发现，也可以在供水系统中形成。
利德贝特说，有一整套关于饮用水分配系统堵塞的环境工程文献。但是，这些材料是如何产生的，原因是什么，仍然是一个谜。显然，许多科学家认为细菌利用锰作为能量可能是罪魁祸首，但是直到现在才有证据支持这一观点。
这一发现有助于研究人员更好地了解地下水的地球化学。众所周知，细菌可以降解地下水中的污染物，这一过程被称为生物修复。在这样做的时候，几个关键的生物体会还原锰氧化物，这意味着它们会向它提供电子，这与人类利用空气中的氧气的方式类似。科学家们想知道氧化锰最初是从哪里来的。
利德贝特说，我们发现的微生物可以产生这种物质，因此他们的生活方式也可以为其他微生物提供所需的物质，以进行我们认为有益和可取的反应。
研究结果也可能与理解散布在大部分海底的锰结核有关。这些圆形的金属球可以和柚子一样大，早在19世纪70年代英国皇家海军挑战者号(HMS Challenger)的巡航中，海洋研究人员就知道了。从那时起，这样的结核被发现排列在地球上许多海洋的底部。近年来，矿业公司一直在制定计划，以收获和开采这些结核，因为在这些结核中经常发现有稀有金属富集。
但对于结核是如何形成的，人们从一开始就知之甚少。Yu和LeadBetter现在想知道，与他们在淡水中发现的微生物相似的微生物是否会起到作用，他们计划进一步调查这一谜团。俞敏洪说，这强调了在采矿摧毁海洋锰结核之前更好地了解它们的必要性。
没有参与这项研究的加州理工大学地球生物学教授伍德沃德·费舍尔(Woodward Fischer)表示，贾里德和亨的这一发现填补了我们对地球元素周期理解的一个重大知识空白，并增加了这种深奥但常见的过渡金属塑造地球生命进化的不同方式。
note_en:
Caltech microbiologists have discovered bacteria that feed on manganese and use the metal as their source of calories. Such microbes were predicted to exist over a century ago, but none had been found or described until now.    "These are the first bacteria found to use manganese as their source of fuel," says Jared Leadbetter, professor of environmental microbiology at Caltech who, in collaboration with postdoctoral scholar Hang Yu, describes the findings in the July 16 issue of the journal  Nature. "A wonderful aspect of microbes in nature is that they can metabolize seemingly unlikely materials, like metals, yielding energy useful to the cell."
  The study also reveals that the bacteria can use manganese to convert carbon dioxide into biomass, a process called chemosynthesis. Previously, researchers knew of bacteria and fungi that could oxidize manganese, or strip it of electrons, but they had only speculated that yet-to-be-identified microbes might be able to harness the process to drive growth.
  Leadbetter found the bacteria serendipitously after performing unrelated experiments using a light, chalk-like form of manganese. He had left a glass jar soiled with the substance to soak in tap water in his Caltech office sink before departing for several months to work off campus. When he returned, the jar was coated with a dark material.
  "I thought, 'What is that?'" he explains. "I started to wonder if long-sought-after microbes might be responsible, so we systematically performed tests to figure that out."
  The black coating was in fact oxidized manganese generated by newfound bacteria that had likely come from the   itself. "There is evidence that relatives of these creatures reside in groundwater, and a portion of Pasadena's drinking water is pumped from local aquifers," he says.
  Manganese is one of the most abundant elements on the surface of the earth. Manganese oxides take the form of a dark, clumpy substance and are common in nature; they have been found in subsurface deposits and can also form in water-distribution systems.
  "There is a whole set of environmental engineering literature on drinking-water-distribution systems getting clogged by  ," says Leadbetter. "But how and for what reason such material is generated there has remained an enigma. Clearly, many scientists have considered that bacteria using manganese for energy might be responsible, but evidence supporting this idea was not available until now."
    The finding helps researchers better understand the geochemistry of groundwater. It is known that bacteria can degrade pollutants in groundwater, a process called bioremediation. When doing this, several key organisms will "reduce" manganese oxide, which means they donate electrons to it, in a manner similar to how humans use oxygen in the air. Scientists have wondered where the manganese oxide comes from in the first place.
  "The   we have discovered can produce it, thus they enjoy a lifestyle that also serves to supply the other microbes with what they need to perform reactions that we consider to be beneficial and desirable," says Leadbetter.
  The research findings also have possible relevance to understanding manganese nodules that dot much of the seafloor. These round metallic balls, which can be as large as grapefruit, were known to marine researchers as early as the cruises of the HMS Challenger in the 1870s. Since then, such nodules have been found to line the bottom of many of Earth's oceans. In recent years, mining companies have been making plans to harvest and exploit these nodules, because rare metals are often found concentrated within them.
  But little is understood about how the nodules form in the first place. Yu and Leadbetter now wonder if microbes similar to what they have found in freshwater might play a role and they plan to further investigate the mystery. "This underscores the need to better understand marine manganese nodules before they are decimated by mining," says Yu.
  "This discovery from Jared and Hang fills a major intellectual gap in our understanding of Earth's elemental cycles, and adds to the diverse ways in which  , an abstruse but common transition metal, has shaped the evolution of life on our planet," says Woodward Fischer, professor of geobiology at Caltech, who was not involved with the study.
title: 在肮脏的玻璃器皿中发现具有金属饮食的细菌
title_en: Bacteria with a metal diet discovered in dirty glassware

note:
欧洲最高法院今天推翻了允许在欧盟运营的公司向美国转移数据的协议。法院裁定，该协议使欧洲客户的数据过于暴露于美国政府的监视之下。
这项名为隐私盾牌(Privacy Shield)的协议自2016年开始实施，有5000多家公司在其条款下运营。归根结底，欧盟法院(CJEU)基本上裁定，美国法律过于薄弱，无法按照欧盟法律的要求保护欧盟公民的数据。正如法院在一份新闻稿中所说的那样(PDF)：
美国国内法对个人数据保护的限制，以及美国公共当局访问和使用从欧盟转移来的此类数据的限制……。不受限制，以满足基本上等同于欧盟法律所要求的要求的方式。
此案的结果是，在欧洲开展业务或处理来自欧洲客户的数据的美国公司要么不得不与欧盟谈判新的个人数据处理安排，即所谓的标准合同条款(SCC)，要么将停止将欧洲业务的数据转移到美国。这项裁决适用于Facebook等公司出于内部原因转移到美国服务器的数据，但它不影响必要的数据传输，比如当欧洲有人向美国的收件人发送电子邮件、在美国网站上预订机票或酒店，或者做同样平常的事情时，就会发生这种情况。
从2000年到2015年，管理欧洲和美国之间共享欧盟客户数据的协议被称为安全港。在奥地利隐私权倡导者马克西米利安·施雷姆斯(Maximillian Schrems)提出法律挑战后，CJEU于2015年宣布安全港无效。在斯诺登爆料后，施雷姆斯声称安全港协议(允许美国国家安全局获取欧盟公民的个人数据)与欧盟法律相冲突。2013年10月，法院同意并宣布安全港框架无效。
在抛出安全港后，欧盟立法者与美国商务部迅速整合了隐私盾牌框架，欧盟委员会于2016年通过了该框架。然而，该框架甚至在立法者投票通过之前就面临着深深的怀疑。欧盟监管机构甚至在该协议正式签署之前就警告称，目前的隐私盾牌还不够强大，不足以承受未来法院的法律审查。
监管机构当时还警告说，隐私盾牌可能与欧洲全面的隐私法--“一般数据保护条例”(GDPR)相冲突。欧盟立法者于2016年通过了这项法律，自2018年以来一直生效。
施雷姆斯在2016年对阿斯开玩笑说，尽管他希望有人提起诉讼，但他个人并不一定对成为这样做的人感兴趣。然而，事实证明，他做到了-CJEU今天裁决的案件通常被称为Schrems II-并再次获胜。
施雷姆斯在CJEU裁决后的一份声明中表示，很明显，如果美国公司想要继续在欧盟市场发挥重要作用，美国将不得不认真修改他们的监控法。这一判决并不是限制数据传输的原因，而是美国监控法的后果。
美国各大科技公司迅速做出保证，表示这项裁决暂时不会大幅改变它们在欧洲的业务，许多公司证实，除了隐私盾牌协议外，它们已经在使用SCC。
微软在一篇公司博客文章中写道，如果你是商业客户，你可以按照欧洲法律继续使用微软的服务。法院的裁决不会改变您今天使用微软云在欧盟和美国之间传输数据的能力。
微软还计划与欧盟委员会和美国政府积极合作，解决该裁决提出的问题，该公司补充道。
Facebook代表伊夫·纳格尔发表声明称，我们欢迎欧盟法院确认向非欧盟国家传输数据的标准合同条款有效性的决定。声明补充说，像许多企业一样，Facebook现在正在仔细考虑这一决定的结果和影响，并期待着在这方面提供监管指导。这份声明补充说，Facebook和许多企业一样，正在仔细考虑这一决定的调查结果和影响。我们期待着在这方面获得监管指导。(注：脸谱网代表伊夫·纳格尔)发表声明称，我们欢迎欧盟法院确认向非欧盟国家传输数据的标准合同条款的有效性。声明补充说，Facebook和许多企业一样，目前正在仔细考虑这一决定的结果和影响，并期待在这方面获得监管指导。
科技行业团体不失时机地呼吁美国和欧盟监管机构迅速制定一个强有力的监管框架，以取代现已不复存在的隐私盾牌(Privacy Shield)。
代表全球5000多家应用开发商的应用协会(App Association)主席摩根·里德(Morgan Reed)表示，隐私盾牌的崩溃将对中小企业造成不成比例的影响，这些企业占使用隐私盾牌公司的70%。这一决定使数千家美国和欧盟公司得不到亟需的数据共享机制，并将严重扰乱价值数千亿美元的跨大西洋数据市场。我们敦促欧盟和美国政府尽快就替代方案进行谈判，以保证所有使用跨境数据传输的企业在法律上的确定性，并确保跨大西洋数据经济的持续增长。
代表亚马逊(Amazon)、Facebook和谷歌(Google)等你听说过的许多大型科技公司的计算机与通信行业协会(Computer&Amp；Communications Industry Association)也表达了同样的观点。CCIA公共政策高级经理亚历山大·劳雷(Alexandre Roure)表示，这一决定给大西洋两岸数千家依赖隐私盾牌进行日常商业数据传输的大小公司带来了法律上的不确定性。我们相信，欧盟和美国的决策者将根据欧盟法律，迅速制定一个可持续的解决方案，以确保支撑跨大西洋经济的数据流持续存在。
title: 美欧隐私盾牌数据共享协议被法院推翻
title_en: US-EU Privacy Shield data sharing agreement struck down by court

note:
随着以音频为中心的平台吸引投资者的兴趣，旧的虚拟现实创始人正试图推动3D音频作为下一步的发展，以一种与今天的语音聊天平台完全不同的方式展示这项技术。尽管其中一些努力已经酝酿了一段时间，但随着俱乐部等社交活动的迅速发展和投资者继续吞并音频初创公司，羽翼未丰的平台要有趣得多。
它们还建立在苹果最近宣布将为AirPods Pro带来3D音频支持的基础上，这一功能可以让身临其境的音频平台进一步推向真实感。
3D音频是一种技术，它可以操纵用户听到的声音，使其与他们的耳朵(或化身)相对于那些制造噪音的物体的位置相对应。这项技术给用户带来了更好的空间存在感，到目前为止，在已经推出自己的3D音频工具包的公司中，这项技术主要用于改善与谷歌、Facebook和Valve的AR/VR体验。
随着越来越多的初创公司寻求利用这项技术，他们发现，根据你与屏幕上另一位用户的距离调整音频可以非常容易地离开和加入群组，就像人们在现实世界对话中一样，在应用程序中进行群集。
note_en:
As audio-centric platforms garner investor interest, virtual reality founders of old are trying to push 3D audio as the next evolution, presenting the tech in a way that looks entirely different from today’s voice chat platforms. Though some of these efforts have been in the works for a while, the fledgling platforms are a lot more interesting, as social efforts like Clubhouse take flight and investors continue to eat up audio startups.
 They also build on Apple’s recent announcement that it’s bringing 3D audio support to the AirPods Pro, a feature which could allow immersive audio platforms to push even further toward realism.
 3D audio is a technology that manipulates the sounds a user is hearing to correspond with where their ears (or an avatar’s) are positioned relative to those noise-making objects. The tech gives users a greater sense of spatial presence and has, to date, been primarily used to improve AR/VR experiences with Google, Facebook and Valve among the companies that have shipped their own 3D audio toolkits.
 As more startups look to leverage the tech, they’re finding that adjusting audio based on how close you’re getting to another user onscreen can make it pretty easy to leave and join groups, clustering in apps like people would in real-world conversations.
title: 沉浸式聊天初创公司对语音的未来有着截然不同的愿景
title_en: Immersive chat startups have a very different vision for the future of voice

note:
冥想应用程序一直是许多人以科技为中心的喘息之机，因为世界继续四分五裂。HBO Max希望在这一成功的基础上，推出一部基于广受欢迎的Calm应用程序的新节目。谢天谢地，它既不是戏剧性的，也不是职场喜剧(HBO已经做了创业的事情)，而是一种不同的格式，更符合你将在应用程序上获得的内容。
这部剧的格式是一季10集半小时的剧集，松散地围绕着凯姆的睡眠故事系列建立起来，视觉效果来自国家地理的One Strange Rock纪录片系列的制片人。这是“科学设计的叙事”的背景，好莱坞一线明星阵容相当扎实，包括马赫莎拉·阿里(Mahershala Ali)、伊德里斯·艾尔巴(Idris Elba)、奥斯卡·艾萨克(Oscar Isaac)、妮可·基德曼(Nicole Kidman)、佐埃·克拉维茨(ZoëKravitz)、露西·刘(Lucy Liu)、西利安·墨菲(Cillian Murphy。
Calm联合创始人兼联合首席执行官迈克尔·阿克顿·史密斯在一份新闻稿中表示：“Calm最初是一款冥想应用程序，但这个品牌已经演变得远远超过了这一点。”他说：“我们很高兴首次将我们的音频睡眠故事背后的魔力搬上银幕。这些体验是视觉上的镇静剂，将帮助人们在这些紧张的时刻放松和放松。“。
这一概念与Netflix的壁炉视频或成人游泳的乔·佩拉告诉你睡眠没有什么不同，因为它们扩展了标准的流媒体服务，将电视连续剧和电影的用途重新调整为这种格式。像HBO Max这样的服务的点播性质使得提供更多的专业内容成为可能，比方说，睡着了。Quibi也有自己的冥想项目--每日寒意。例如，这些天来，我对着Netflix入睡的次数比我愿意承认的要多得多。
note_en:
Meditation apps have been a nice tech-centric respite for many, as the world continues to fall apart at the seams. HBO Max is hoping to build on that success with the launch of a new show based on the wildly popular Calm app. It is, thankfully, neither drama nor workplace comedy (HBO’s already done the startup thing), but rather a different format more inline with what you’ll get on the app.
 Formatted as a season of 10 half-hour episodes, the show is loosely built around Calm’s sleep Stories series, with visuals from the producers of National Geographic’s One Strange Rock documentary series. That serves as the backdrop to “scientifically-engineered narratives” read by a pretty solid cast of Hollywood A-Listers, including Mahershala Ali, Idris Elba, Oscar Isaac, Nicole Kidman, Zoë Kravitz, Lucy Liu, Cillian Murphy and Keanu Reeves.
  “Calm started life as a meditation app but the brand has evolved far beyond that,” Calm co-founder and co-CEO Michael Acton Smith said in a release. “We are delighted to bring the magic behind our audio Sleep Stories to the screen for the first time. These experiences are visual Valium and will help people relax and unwind during these stressful times.”
 The concept isn’t dissimilar from Netflix’s fireplace videos or Adult Swim’s Joe Pera Talks You  to Sleep, in that they extend the standard streaming service play of repurposing television series and films for the format. The on-demand nature of services like HBO Max make it possible to offer up more specialty content to, say, fall asleep to. Quibi, too, has its own meditation program, The Daily Chill. I, for one, have been falling asleep to Netflix much more often than I’d care to admit, these days.
title: 平静冥想应用程序正在推出自己的名人云集的HBO Max秀
title_en: The Calm meditation app is getting its own celebrity-filled HBO Max show

note:
(路透社)-两名知情人士告诉路透社，Twitter Inc.最近几周加大了对首席信息安全官的搜索力度，周三高调账户被入侵引发了人们对该平台安全的担忧。
美国联邦调查局(FBI)旧金山分部正在领导对Twitter黑客事件的调查，因为更多华盛顿议员呼吁对事件发生的原因进行解释。
该执法机构表示，黑客在夺取了乔·拜登(Joe Biden)、金·卡戴珊(Kim Kardashian)、巴拉克·奥巴马(Barack Obama)和埃隆·马斯克(Elon Musk)等名人和政治人物的Twitter账户后，实施了加密货币欺诈。
入侵事件发生一天后，尚不清楚黑客是否能够看到账户持有人发送的私人信息。美国议员对未来的袭击感到担忧。
美国民主党参议员埃德·马基(Ed Markey)在一份声明中表示：“虽然这一计划似乎是出于经济动机…想象一下，如果这些坏行为者有不同的意图，利用强大的声音传播虚假信息，潜在地干预我们的选举，扰乱股市，或扰乱我们的国际关系，”美国民主党参议员埃德·马基(Ed Markey)在一份声明中说。
众议院司法委员会(House Judiciary Committee)共和党领袖、众议员吉姆·乔丹(Jim Jordan)呼应了类似的情绪，他问道，如果Twitter允许类似事件在11月2日，也就是美国总统大选的前一天发生，会发生什么。
乔丹说，截至周四下午，他仍然被锁在Twitter账户之外，并表示他对公司运营方式的信心一直在恶化。
发言人凯莉·麦克纳尼(Kayleigh McEnany)表示，唐纳德·特朗普(Donald Trump)总统是一名多产的推特用户，他计划继续发推文，他的账户在袭击期间是安全的。
她说，白宫“在过去的18个小时里一直在与推特保持联系”，以确保特朗普的推特订阅的安全。
Twitter表示，黑客瞄准了有权访问其内部系统的员工，并“利用这种访问权限，代表他们控制了许多高度可见(包括经过验证的)账户和推文”。
其他被黑客攻击的高调账户包括说唱歌手坎耶·韦斯特、亚马逊公司创始人杰夫·贝佐斯、投资者沃伦·巴菲特、微软公司联合创始人比尔·盖茨，以及优步技术公司和苹果公司的公司账户。
该公司自去年12月以来一直没有安全主管，该公司表示，黑客对其一些拥有内部系统访问权限的员工进行了“协调的社会工程攻击”。
在调查这起入侵事件时，它采取了一个非常的步骤，暂时阻止了许多经过验证的账户发布信息。
被劫持的账户在推特上发布消息，告诉用户发送比特币，他们的钱将翻一番。公开的区块链记录显示，明显的骗子获得了价值超过10万美元的加密货币。
周三，Twitter首席执行官杰克·多尔西(Jack Dorsey)在推特上表示，对Twitter的每个人来说，这是“艰难的一天”，并承诺“当我们对到底发生了什么有了更完整的了解时，我们会尽我们所能”。
多尔西的保证并没有缓解华盛顿对社交媒体公司的担忧，这些公司的政策受到了左翼和右翼批评人士的密切关注。
据一位不愿透露姓名的委员会官员透露，民主党参议员马克·华纳(Mark Warner)呼吁Twitter和执法部门调查此事，而美国众议院情报委员会(US House Intelligence Committee)表示，它正在就黑客攻击与Twitter联系。
共和党参议员乔希·霍利(Josh Hawley)在黑客袭击发生几分钟后就给多尔西写了一封信，询问潜在的数据被盗情况，以及此次入侵是否影响了选定的用户或平台的整体安全。
众议院能源和商务委员会(House Energy And Commerce Committee)主席、民主党人弗兰克·帕隆(Frank Pallone)在一条推文中表示，该公司“需要解释所有这些知名账户是如何被黑客入侵的。”该委员会负责监督美国相当大一部分的科技政策。
纽约州金融服务部(New York State Department Of Financial Services)也加入进来，表示将调查黑客攻击事件。
note_en:
(Reuters) - Twitter Inc had stepped up its search for a chief information security officer in recent weeks, two people familiar with the search told Reuters, before the breach of high-profile accounts on Wednesday raised alarms about the platform’s security.
 The FBI’s San Francisco division is leading an inquiry into the Twitter hacking, as more Washington lawmakers called for an accounting of how it happened.
 The law enforcement agency said hackers committed cryptocurrency fraud after they seized control of the Twitter accounts of celebrities and political figures including Joe Biden, Kim Kardashian, Barack Obama and Elon Musk.
 A day after the breach, it was not clear if the hackers were able to see private messages sent by the account holders. U.S. lawmakers fretted about future attacks.
 “While this scheme appears financially motivated...imagine if these bad actors had a different intent to use powerful voices to spread disinformation to potentially interfere with our elections, disrupt the stock market, or upset our international relations,” U.S. Senator Ed Markey, a Democrat, said in a statement.
 Echoing a similar sentiment, Representative Jim Jordan, the top Republican on the House Judiciary Committee, asked what would happen if Twitter allowed a similar incident to occur on Nov. 2, a day before the U.S. presidential election.
 Jordan said he remained locked out of his Twitter account as of Thursday afternoon and said his confidence in how the company operates has been deteriorating.
 President Donald Trump, a prolific Twitter user, was planning to continue tweeting and his account was secure during the attack, spokeswoman Kayleigh McEnany said.
 The White House had been in “constant contact with Twitter over the last 18 hours” to keep Trump’s Twitter feed secure, she said.
  Twitter said hackers had targeted employees with access to its internal systems and “used this access to take control of many highly-visible (including verified) accounts and Tweet on their behalf”.
 Other high-profile accounts that were hacked included rapper Kanye West, Amazon.com Inc founder Jeff Bezos, investor Warren Buffett, Microsoft Corp co-founder Bill Gates, and the corporate accounts for Uber Technologies Inc and Apple Inc.
 The company, which has been without a security chief since December, said hackers conducted a “coordinated social engineering attack” against some of its employees with access to internal systems.
 In an extraordinary step, it temporarily prevented many verified accounts from publishing messages as it investigated the breach.
 The hijacked accounts tweeted out messages telling users to send bitcoin and their money would be doubled. Publicly available blockchain records show that the apparent scammers received more than $100,000 worth of cryptocurrency.
  CEO Jack Dorsey said in a tweet on Wednesday that it was a “tough day” for everyone at Twitter and pledged to share “everything we can when we have a more complete understanding of exactly what happened”.
 Dorsey’s assurances did not assuage Washington’s concerns about social media companies, whose policies have come under scrutiny by critics on both the left and the right.
 Democratic Senator Mark Warner called on Twitter and law enforcement to investigate the matter while the U.S. House Intelligence Committee said it was in touch with Twitter regarding the hack, according to a committee official who did not wish to be named.
  Republican Senator Josh Hawley wrote a letter to Dorsey within minutes of the hack and asked about potential data theft and whether the breach affected select users or the security of the platform overall.
 Frank Pallone, a Democrat who chairs the House Energy and Commerce Committee that oversees a sizeable portion of U.S. tech policy, said in a tweet the company “needs to explain how all of these prominent accounts were hacked.”
 The New York State Department of Financial Services also weighed in, saying it will investigate the hack.
